profile.statement_to_voters:
Hi, I’m Cameron, the Green Party’s candidate for Medlock Vale. I’m one of Oldham Green Party’s Youth Officers, a youth worker a local Community Interest Company, an ex-GCSE tutor, and last but not least, a young man passionate about creating a better future for our community.

I’ve lived in the Medlock Vale ward for most of my life, and have seen a lot of change within my 18 years of living here- I’ve seen a decrease in services, a loss of community spirit, and most unfortunately, I’ve seen a cut in funding for youth services. That’s just one of my reasons for standing as the Green Party candidate for our ward, and there are many more, which (if you ask me) I’d love to tell you about.

I’m hoping that I’ll be able to be elected as the councillor for our ward, and that through this I’m going to get the best possible results for our community; an increase in funding for our youth services, strengthened community ties, and giving our residents someone that will listen. I look forward to hearing from you, and I can’t wait to see what I can do to help!

Promoted by Cameron Lee Gates (Green Party), at Flat 2, 56 Gainsborough Avenue, Oldham, OL8 1AJ.

question: Who will win the Medlock Vale ward in the Oldham local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters: Sally Kemp has lived in Broxbourne Borough all her life, attending school in Cheshunt before studying economics at Newcastle University. Her main career interest has been primary teaching, as a special needs coordinator and organiser of environmental studies. She has also served as a school governor. Sally's particular local concern is preserving open spaces in the borough both for local people to enjoy and to enhance biodiversity.
I live in the Borough of Broxbourne and feel it is important for everyone to be able to vote for a representative of the party they want to.So I am happy to stand as a Lib Dem and offer that option.

I'm Mark and I'm proud to be a resident of the Borough since the age of two weeks. I went to St Paul's, Haslewood (until they merged into st caths) and John Warner. Learnt to swim at the old lido. Had my first paying job here at Pindar road and despite the millennial "difficulty mode" at life I'm also a proud homeowner in hoddesdon.

I've been involved with the community as a teenager since my time with East Herts Scout with events such as the deconsfield fate, and in recent years the local Conservative party which I do believe wholeheartedly has been fantastic for the Borough despite the parliamentary picture in the last few years. As someone who has benefited from the local services (especially the local SEND provision) my main mission is to give back to the community.

There's the "why", now the "How"! The mission is simple, low taxes and good services. The council has maintained one of the lowest borough council tax in the country (only a extra 10p a week for Band D properties this year) whilst keeping to the biweekly bin collection routine, regular gardening of public spaces (there has been comments on this in my canvassing sessions, has not been forgotten!), local council owned leisure faculties such as John Warner and Laura Trott sports centres and events like rock around the clock in hoddesdon. We want to continue on this route and where there's issues, your councillors will always hear you and have your back.

If you have any concerns please get in touch, as campaigning season is getting into full swing I'm out most afternoon delivering materials in the ward so if you see me feel welcome to say hi and have a chat!

question: Who will win the Northgate & West Green ward in the Crawley local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
LET’S KEEP OUR AREA SPECIAL I have  lived at Fairplace Farm, Nutley since 1995 with my wife Helen. We have brought up two children here, one of whom is a Consultant in emergency medicine, so we know from her the pressures the NHS is facing. Our son is a successful Quantity Surveyor. We have three grandsons who love nature and enjoy adventures in the Forest. Neither Helen nor I want to see creeping urbanization of our beautiful area.  If elected I will oppose the application for the expansion of the Ashdown Business Park and applications for substantial further residential development in the surrounding areas.

ENOUGH IS ENOUGH There has been enough building, and the infrastructure cannot cope with more.  Concreting and tarmacking over more and more land causes more problems with flooding and further releases of sewage into our rivers. It increasingly produces problems with power generation.

I may not always agree with you but I will always fight your corner!

I have lived in the Uckfield area and been married, for 30 years. We have two ‘grown up’ children that have both flown the nest and we have a granddaughter. We have three Scottish Terriers that tend to ignore anything that I say and I have been trying unsuccessfully to learn to play the guitar for fifty years.

I am state educated and had no idea what a university was when I was eighteen.

For the last thirteen years, together with my wife, we owned and ran a successful care business in Sussex.

From 2019 – 2023, I served as a Conservative District Councillor (Wealden).

I have always enjoyed volunteering. Many years ago, I was an army reservist. I spent 6 years as part of the RNLI as a crew member on the Newhaven Lifeboat and have also been a Special Constable based at Haywards Heath.

Why am I standing for election and why for the Reform Party?

I am certainly not a career politician but over the last couple of years, I have realised that I no longer have synergy with any of the major political parties in the UK. Effectively, there seems to be too much politics in politics. We have a short-term political system whereby political parties don't have the courage to make the radical changes that this country needs if we are to progress. We need radical change and need to be brave to make those changes. We are a great nation but we need to drive forward. Proportional representation and more cross party working to utilise all talent in British politics would be a good start.

Business

Running a business is tough. Making decisions that affect hundreds of people whilst continuing to keep your business viable is not easy. I believe that SMEs play a huge part in driving the economy whilst providing jobs to many people. Reform will reduce the amount of Corporation Tax paid by businesses and increase the income tax threshold to encourage people off benefits and back to work.

NHS

I support the NHS. My daughter was born in the Princess Royal Hospital and spent two weeks in the special care unit so we are eternally grateful for all the support given at that time. Unfortunately, much has changed over the years and our NHS is now in crisis. There is no point in ploughing more money into a system that isn't working before significant changes are made. Reform has clearly set out how those changes can be made to give the NHS a second life.

Police

I have had the pleasure of working with Sussex Police as a special constable and understand the challenges and frustrations felt by many police officers. County drug lines, rural crime, antisocial behaviour and shop lifting are all on the increase in the local area. We do need more police officers, but we also need to streamline processes and procedures, so officers spend less time in the station and more time on patrol. This is what most officers would far rather be doing. Zero tolerance policing and increased stop and search powers will reduce antisocial behaviour and knife crime. We need significantly more detention places so more custodial sentences become the norm and reduced prison sentences due to capacity issues are irradicated. The Reform Party has set out how it will give the police more powers as well as 40,000 additional police officers in the next five years.

Family

Not everything is the fault of the government or the responsibility of schools.

I am a big believer in the family unit (traditional or otherwise).

Reform has set out how it will support families via tax incentives and changes to child benefits.

Housing Crisis

As a district councillor, I spent many years on planning committees and became extremely frustrated with the planning system. The current system demands planning applications for thousands of houses to be passed in the hope that 35% will be affordable to first time buyers or purchased by housing associations. It simply isn't working. We have been left with planning permissions being granted but then being sold on rather than being built out. Those that are built out in reasonable time tend to lack infrastructure. The housing crisis needs defining and then a joined-up approach needs to be formulated. Reform is committed to reviewing the planning system.

Defence

I believe in our commitment to NATO and Trident. As an ex-reservist, I recognise that our army has some of the best trained soldiers in the world, but we are far from the best equipped. Reform is committed to an increase in Defence spending and maintaining a lead role in NATO.

Immigration

As an ex-lifeboat crew member, I have every sympathy for those in danger at sea but we cannot continue to allow the small boats to cross the channel.

Smashing the gangs will not work. The gangs are only providing a service.

Ultimately, we should detain, process and deport illegal immigrants swiftly which will deter and greatly reduce the numbers attempting the crossing.

We also need to work smarter with our European partners to try and stop immigration Europe wide.

Reform has set out its 6-point plan to stop the boats.

Our Contract With You - Reform UK (reformparty.uk)

I will be standing at the next General Election and if successful, I intend to be a strong voice that represents the people of Mid Sussex. If unsuccessful, I will be buying a sailing boat and taking guitar lessons.

I will be delighted to hear from you and will welcome any thoughts, comments or frustrations.

Thank you

Tim believes strong, committed county councillors are essential to a healthy democracy, particularly at a time when politics can feel increasingly divisive. He is committed to working hard for all residents and ensuring local voices are heard. If elected, Tim will focus on ensuring that the council is improving transport links, maintaining highways and rural roads, supporting village schools with adapting to changes to SEND and school organisation in the government’s Education Bill, and, importantly, working with partner organisations to ensure that young people are supported with that first step into a career.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I have had strong ties to St Sidwells and St James for around 50 years, with my children all attending Newtown first school, two of whom are dyslexic and one autistic, and now all 3 are hard working, tax paying, family men.

I left home at 16, married at 17, was widowed at 22, had lengthy periods of unemployment, returned to education in my late 20s, became a self employed taxi driver and started a property business.  I now manage the properties and also work on a zero hours contract for a Royal Mail Group company.  

I have worked hard to make ends meet and to become financially sustainable, and have even been a trade union member for most of my working life.  I can see, however that although the mainstream parties have now abandoned my families’ values and any form of common sense, I am thrilled that the Reform UK Party has not!

My work in providing housing for local people has enabled me to be passionate about local issues such as parking, taxation, social care and special needs education!  I want to offer the electorate an alternative vision of common-sense local governance that directly benefits the entire community and supports local values rather than out-of-touch ideologies which only cater to a few.

question: Who will win the Newtown & St Leonard's ward in the Exeter local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
I live with my family in the heart of Havenstreet village and already serve the community as Chair or our Parish Council. Most significantly I’ve spearheaded a campaign to get Havenstreet back on the bus network and represented resident’s interests in dealing with the all-too frequent disruption caused by roadworks in the village and surrounding area. This ward, one of the island’s largest, is a mix of larger villages and small hamlets. There are many thriving communities and businesses which deserve a representative at County Hall who will put their interests first at every opportunity.
My career in the British Army has given me wide experience of leading teams, managing change and budgets whilst being accountable for delivering the required outcomes. Just the skills needed on our council.
I volunteer as a Director/Trustee of the Isle of Wight Steam Railway, one of the Island’s top heritage and tourist attractions and a significant local employer. I fully understand the challenges currently faced by all Island businesses and the tourist sector in particular, a thriving tourist economy is absolutely vital for our Island. I strongly believe that our council must do everything it can to support all local businesses.
I am standing to be your Isle of Wight councillor because I believe that it’s time for change in County Hall. The traditional parties and the ‘Independents’ have had their chance. Reform UK is ready to step in and deal with the issues facing us, starting with the council’s finances which are in a dire state – fresh thinking and a push to further drive down costs, whilst delivering the services our residents need and deserve, is urgently required.
I’m ready to play my part in delivering the common-sense solutions that the Island needs. I’m right behind Reform UK’s core values – Family, Community, Country, and am prepared to work hard to serve all members of our Island community with honesty and integrity.

profile.statement_to_voters:
The Green Party candidate in Rugby on July 4th is Becca Stevenson. She manages 5 Acre Farm, a local community supported farm located near Ryton on Dunsmore whilst also being a director and regional organizer for the Land Workers Alliance, a union supporting agroecological farmers. Becca spends her spare time as a volunteer for Transition Town Rugby and set up Rugby Litter Busters three years ago.

Becca’s campaign foregrounds a fresh, hopeful approach in our politics. In practice that means improving the lives of residents through real investment in the NHS and social care systems on which so many of us rely. Likewise, we need to provide far more affordable and social housing, helping young people in particular to gain their independence. It is also vital that together with tackling further climate change, we counter the effects already with us such as the repeated flooding in the Borough.

Becca commented: “I am excited and honoured to be the Green Party candidate for Rugby in this General Election. I have used my experience in environmental activism and business to forge excellent links with a wide range of groups and I am committed to engaging as many people as possible. I want to be another Green voice in parliament representing real hope and real change for the people of Rugby. “

It looks highly likely that there will be a Labour government after July 4th. In that case, as a Green MP Becca will work to ensure that Labour are ambitious in their aims and don’t simply tweak the current broken system. She will be an effective and principled MP who works to strengthen Rugby’s local economy and protect its natural environment.

Rachel has been the Moortown ward candidate since 2019, came 2nd place in the last local election in 2024 and is looking forward to standing again on May 7th 2026.

Rachel set up the successful campaign group Healthy Air Leeds, and fundraised for the new play area as a committee member of the Meanwood Park Playground Appeal.

Rachel is a long standing member of the local Litter Free Meanwood group and is a big supporter of the wonderful independent businesses we have in Meanwood and Moortown.

“I am passionate about keeping Meanwood and Moortown clean and green, with safe, accessible transport and active travel routes for all.”

Sharon Slinger is the Liberal Democrat candidate for Moortown, known locally for getting stuck in and working alongside residents to make things happen.

She lives in Meanwood with her family and is an active part of the local community. Sharon runs her own small business and is a Fellow of the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors, bringing a strong sense of professionalism and accountability to everything she does.

Sharon has been closely involved in local campaigns, including pushing for action on traffic issues at Meanwood junction and supporting residents in successfully challenging proposals that would have negatively impacted the area. She is also a visible presence locally - reporting and following up on graffiti, raising everyday concerns, and making sure residents’ voices are heard.

Rather than just raising issues, Sharon focuses on getting results. She works with residents, asks the right questions, and keeps pressure on decision-makers until action is taken. Her approach is practical and persistent - not just promises, but follow-through.

For Moortown, Sharon would bring:

* A strong track record of listening to residents and acting on what matters to them
* A hands-on approach to local issues, from traffic and road safety to environmental concerns
* Determination to see things through and get outcomes, not just responses
* A commitment to being visible, accessible, and accountable as a local councillor

Sharon is standing to ensure Moortown has a councillor who works hard, stays engaged with the community, and focuses on delivering real improvements for local people.

I grew up in Woodlesford and still live here, so I know this area well and hold it very close to my heart. I’m standing because I want to be a strong representative for people in Rothwell, Carlton, Oulton and Woodlesford, particularly those who are struggling with the cost of living and feeling left behind by the Labour government.

Poor phone signal is also a major issue for many local residents, particularly those living in the Oulton area.

Too often areas like ours are overlooked and taken for granted. I will be a loud and proud representative for Rothwell on Leeds City Council.

Former Conservative and former Conservative candidate Trevor is standing on a true reform agenda and a ‘small c’ conservative agenda: reform of the NHS, the judiciary, tax, with a pro-growth economic plan, and a new deal for young people on student debt and on home ownership.

Trevor is campaigning against cuts to the armed forces, the hollowing out of Britain’s defence sector and this government’s shoddy treatment of veterans. He was one of the Brexit Party candidates who stood aside for the Boris Johnson intake of Conservatives at the 2019 election.

In the media and locally Trevor is lobbying passionately for a new kind of bottom-up/bubble-up politics, an open, more ambitious and accessible politics. He speaks out against undemocratic and faceless global organisations taking the place of our democratic institutions.

But now, after more than a decade of the Conservatives the public have become conditioned to accept failure. They’ve become used to ministers blaming everyone but themselves, whether it’s the House of Lords, immigration lawyers, EU bureaucrats or the European Court of Human Rights. In the media and locally Trevor is lobbying passionately for a new kind of bottom-up/bubble-up politics, an open, more ambitious and accessible politics. He speaks out against undemocratic and faceless global organisations taking the place of our democratic institutions.

When not on the campaign trail Trevor can be found enjoying countryside walks or writing books on philosophy, politics and 'conscious capitalism'. He holds a degree in Economics and Public Administration from University of London and a Masters degree in Business Administration.

we live.
However, we face serious challenges and I have
campaigned on several key issues: i) remove sewage from
our rivers; ii) improve our roads and reduce the amount of
potholes and iii) work on a Countywide plan to limit HGVs
using our villages and market towns as cut-throughs.
I have given over £10,000 to WASP (Windrush Against
Sewage Pollution) to ensure that we can effectively monitor
and measure the extent of pollution entering our rivers.
This equipment is vital in order to hold Thames Water to
account for their dumping of raw sewage into our rivers.
I am also heavily involved in the County Council HGV study
to limit the large HGVs using our villages and market towns
as a cut through. This work and data gathering should be
completed in 2025-2026.
We need to support our local farmers and ensure that the
current Inheritance Tax proposal is scrapped. My inbox is
also full of complaints from concerned pensioners who face
cuts in their winter fuel payments and face real hardship in
the winter months often having to face a choice between
heating and eating. This is an unfair policy levied against
the most vulnerable in our society.
With your support on Thursday 1st May, I will continue to
campaign to ensure that West Oxfordshire is a place where
we can all be proud to live, work and grow up in for many
years to come.

Leslie Channon: A Local Voice with National Experience for West Oxfordshire District Council

I’ve called Burford home since 2010.  My children grew up here – from pre-school to secondary – and our family life is rooted in this community: the morning school runs, 
nativity plays at the church, school events, and the strong sense of community that comes with putting down proper roots. My parents rest at Burford Church. This isn’t just where I live, it’s where I belong. 
.
Community-First Representation
As a brain tumour survivor and a mother, life has taught me that a strong community isn’t just a "nice-to-have"—it’s a lifeline.  I also believe in leadership that shows up. When our water mains failed recently, I worked alongside our MP, Charlie Maynard, to get bottled water to neighbours and set up the water station at Burford school.

That is the kind of "sleeves-rolled-up" representation I promise to bring to Burford.

I am a bridge-builder.
In my professional life, I specialise in creating "pitchfork-free" zones—spaces where people with different views can come together to find common ground. Whether I was working with the Government following the Grenfell Tower tragedy or scrutinising local services, my focus has always been ensuring that the people at the heart of the issue are actually heard.

A Professional Advocate for West Oxfordshire
That is why I am standing for West Oxfordshire District Council. I believe I offer both professional expertise and a strong commitment to Burford.

I want to give back to the community that has been so supportive to me and use my experience to deliver collaborative, common-sense solutions for all of us.

I have the professional expertise to handle the "system" and the heart to never forget that I am working for you. I’m not just asking for your vote; I’m asking for the chance to be your voice.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I have lived in Cheshunt for nearly 11 years and I now work in corporate affairs for a national trade association representing the British businesses underpinned by heritage crafts, a vital sector that supports over half a million skilled workers and contributes £81 billion to our economy. These businesses, rooted in generations of tradition, not only preserve our cultural identity but also serve as a powerful tool of British soft power on the world stage. I was born in Romania, but raised with traditional British values of hard work, fairness, and community. values that are deeply reflected in the craft sector. It’s a privilege to advocate for industries that keep these values alive and pass them on through skilled, meaningful employment across the country.

I joined the Labour Party in 2017, driven by a growing frustration with the deepening inequalities in society and the continued erosion of our public services under Conservative leadership. I believe passionately that government should invest in the things that make a real, positive difference in people’s lives: from strong local services to affordable housing, accessible healthcare, and secure, meaningful jobs. Everyone deserves the opportunity to thrive, not just the wealthy few. For me, Labour is the party best placed to rebuild a fairer, more inclusive Britain, one where prosperity is shared and no one is left behind.

I have lived in Cheshunt all my life and care deeply about the future of our community. I am standing as a Green Party candidate because I believe local politics should focus on supporting residents, bringing people together, and protecting the environment we all rely on.

I work in IT in the public transport sector, supporting essential public services, and understand the importance of practical, reliable systems that work for everyday people. Outside of work, I enjoy walking and bird watching and regularly visit local nature reserves, which has strengthened my commitment to protecting green spaces.

I am standing to offer residents a positive and compassionate alternative in local politics, with a focus on protecting green spaces, tackling fly tipping, improving local services, and supporting safer neighbourhoods in Rosedale and Bury Green.

I believe local representatives should be approachable, listen to residents, and work constructively to improve the community.

I have recently moved into Wallsend North, in order to be closer to my family. It has not taken me long to feel at home and totally committed to my new community. 
I have a deep desire to contribute to the well-being of Wallsend and being involved in the Green Party is an important way of demonstrating this. 
I believe wholeheartedly that local citizens should have a real say in what happens in their neighbourhood because they know it best. In representing Wallsend North, I would make sure that I listen thoughtfully and meaningfully to the voices of local people, in order to represent them on North Tyneside council.
I have worked for 40+ years in local authority children's services, a national children's charity and as an independent trainer. Prior to this, I was a nurse in the NHS.
The reason I am an active member of the Green Party is that I believe in its values of social and environmental justice, as it works hard to improve the everyday quality of life of ordinary people - not just the privileged few. I already know that it often takes courage to speak up for these values, particularly when confronted by messages of hate, division and cynicism in the wider political arena.
But I don't let this put me off. On the contrary, it makes me more determined to reach out to my local community with warmth and a genuine interest in the stories and lived experience of people living in Wallsend North. 
It can only be a good thing for North Tyneside council to have new voices with positive messages of hope and optimism, and constructive challenge, woven into its decision making for local communities, including Wallsend North. 
I am determined that I would be such a voice, on behalf of my community.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I’m standing for the Green Party because it has values that I share: caring for people, caring for the environment, and promoting local voices and local action. I worked in the NHS for 20 years as a psychologist and am acutely aware of the damage the pandemic has done to the mental health of children and adults. I know levels of loneliness in our society have increased and poverty is on the rise. And these are also bad for our mental health. I want us to work together to combat poor mental health on a community level.

Access to green spaces and connections with other people are beneficial for wellbeing so I want to see these protected and enhanced in our patch. I’m already a Tree-Keeper for Perry Common Meadows Tiny Forest. I want to see the people of Perry Common, Short Heath and the New Oscott estate develop our own local plan in partnership with the council to protect the quality of life of us all.

My name is Ahmed Ghulam, and I am standing as an Independent candidate for Perry Common Ward because I believe our community deserves a stronger, more practical, and more accountable local voice.

I have lived and worked in Birmingham for many years and have built my professional career around solving real problems, managing projects responsibly, and working directly with residents and communities. I am not affiliated with any political party — I am standing independently so that I can represent you, not party interests.

My priorities are simple, realistic, and focused on what matters most to local people.

My commitments to you:
	•	To listen to residents and act on their concerns
	•	To be visible, accessible, and accountable throughout the year — not just at election time
	•	To work constructively with the council, the police, and community groups to improve daily life in Perry Common
	•	To always be honest about what the council can and cannot do, and how public money is being spent

My key priorities for Perry Common:
	1.	Safer streets and neighbourhoods
	•	Work with West Midlands Police and local safety teams to improve visible policing and response times
	•	Tackle anti-social behaviour, fly-tipping, and nuisance activity that affects residents’ quality of life
	•	Support better lighting, CCTV where appropriate, and safer public spaces
	2.	Clean, well-maintained neighbourhoods
	•	Push for regular street cleaning, quicker waste collection responses, and stronger action against fly-tipping
	•	Ensure parks, pavements, and public spaces are properly maintained
	•	Hold contractors and the council accountable for poor-quality work
	3.	Fair use of public money
	•	Challenge wasteful spending and demand transparency in council decisions
	•	Support investment that delivers real value for residents, not just paperwork and consultation
	•	Ensure local services are funded where they make the biggest difference
	4.	Support for families, older residents, and the vulnerable
	•	Protect access to essential services such as social care, housing support, and community facilities
	•	Ensure residents can access help easily and are not passed between departments
	•	Speak up for those who do not always feel heard
	5.	Local voices at the heart of decision-making
	•	Regular surgeries and open communication with residents
	•	Work with community groups, faith groups, schools, and volunteers
	•	Ensure Perry Common is not overlooked or taken for granted

Why I am standing

I am standing because too many residents feel that their concerns are ignored, that decisions are made without proper consultation, and that accountability is lacking. I believe Perry Common deserves a councillor who is:
	•	Present in the community
	•	Willing to challenge poor decisions
	•	Focused on practical outcomes, not political point-scoring

I will work respectfully with all parties and organisations where it benefits residents, but I will never hesitate to speak up when something is wrong.

A clear promise

If elected, I promise to:
	•	Represent all residents fairly, regardless of background or political views
	•	Communicate openly and regularly about what I am doing on your behalf
	•	Always put the interests of Perry Common first

Thank you for taking the time to read my statement. I would be honoured to have your support.

Ahmed Ghulam
Independent Candidate — Perry Common Ward

profile.statement_to_voters:
I am standing for Pantiles ward because I care deeply about Tunbridge Wells and want to play a more active role in shaping its future. Through my work with the Green Weald Alliance and local residents, I have already been engaging with key issues affecting our community, from road safety along Frant Road to the proposed development at St Mark’s and the concerns around Ramslye Farm. I have also been following local issues such as the future of the Colonnade and how it is managed for residents and businesses. I have been acting in this space because these issues matter, but I am now seeking a clear mandate to do more and to represent residents formally.

We need development that is responsible, sustainable, and genuinely aligned with local needs. Building executive homes on green spaces does not solve affordability challenges or infrastructure pressures, and it risks damaging the character and heritage of our area, including important historical assets. I will advocate for a brownfield and vacant homes first approach, protect our green spaces, and ensure that residents have a strong, credible voice in the decisions that affect them.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I’m Taila, and I’m standing in the upcoming County Council election for the Attleborough Division.

I was born and raised in Attleborough, and I’ve been proud to serve our community for over 10 years — currently as Deputy Mayor and as a Breckland District Councillor for the Attleborough Burgh & Haverscroft Ward. Public service runs in my family, and I’m proud to be the third generation to serve our town.

My biggest inspiration is my daughter, Paisley. As a mum to an autistic child, I understand how challenging it can be for families to access the right support, which is why improving SEND services and building a kinder, more inclusive community means so much to me.

I’m a councillor who believes in doing the right thing — not the easy thing. I’ve always stepped up when our town needed it, whether it was organising community support during the pandemic or helping residents directly during the 2023 floods. I care deeply about this place and the people in it, and I’ll always roll up my sleeves when needed.

My priorities:

• Making sure Attleborough gets the infrastructure it needs as we grow

• Improving SEND support for children, young people and adults

• Working to reduce flood risk in Attleborough and Besthorpe

• Ensuring public money is used wisely and effectively

If I’m elected, you’ll have a strong, consistent voice representing you across all three levels of local government — someone who will always listen and always fight for what’s best for our community, championing the needs of Attleborough and Besthorpe every step of the way.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Sam first moved to Cambridge for work 6 years ago, and immediately fell in love with Petersfield. He has lived here with his partner ever since.

Sam is a full-time campaigner, with experience in winning change for local people.

Sam lives in the heart of Petersfield and understands the issues - local and national - facing our community, and its businesses, pubs and green spaces.

Sam says, "If elected I will stand up for our city’s values and will work with other Lib Dems to hold Labour to account on issues like Europe and Gaza.

Contact Sam: samnjoliver97@gmail.com

Hello, I'm Cameron, the Labour leader of Cambridge City Council, and I would love to have the opportunity to serve as a councillor in Petersfield ward. I’m standing because I care deeply about improving people’s lives and building a fairer, kinder society. Cllr Richard Robertson will be stepping down this year — he has been an incredibly dedicated and effective councillor, and I hope that I can go some way to following in his footsteps.

I've lived and worked in Petersfield, and I want to make sure that, as our city grows, we protect and improve amenities and quality of life for Petersfield residents.
Many residents have raised with me concerns about crime and antisocial behaviour. I have a professional background in prison education, and will use my knowledge of crime prevention to work with police and residents on issues such as drug dealing and shoplifting.

We all want to improve the state of our roads and pavements, and to make it safer to walk and cycle. For Mill Road specifically, I will work with local residents, business owners and fellow councillors to try to find a way forward that can gain broad-based consensus and make Mill Road a safer, more pleasant place to be.

I love the diverse, welcoming nature of Petersfield’s communities. As a modern languages graduate, I’m committed to promoting multiculturalism.
Homelessness is a key issue I am working to tackle in Petersfield and across Cambridge. Over the last year, I have co-chaired and supported the development of the Cambridge Homelessness Charter, a new alliance of councils, charities, businesses and universities working to reduce homelessness as far as possible.

The City Council recently stopped using herbicides, to help butterflies, bees and other pollinators to thrive, but we do need to clear weeds where necessary. The Labour-run City Council has just put an extra £80,000 into clearing weeds in the next two years.
 
As Leader of the City Council, I’m proud that Cambridge is leading the world in medical advances that save millions of lives, and want to help to create the conditions for that to continue. But we must ensure that our successful economy works for local residents, with affordable housing, job opportunities, public services and facilities for existing and new communities, new transport infrastructure and a healthy environment.

As a Labour administration, we are doing our utmost to reduce poverty and inequality in Cambridge, and to urgently tackle the climate and biodiversity emergencies. 
We’ve built over 700 desperately-needed council homes, with plans for 1300 more in the next decade; hiked council tax on second homes and empty homes in the city; helped to set up Greater Cambridge Impact, an innovative social investment fund which has already raised over £6m to tackle poverty and inequality; we’re working on Included, an ambitious new programme aimed at making sure that every young person in Cambridge has real access to skills, training, and good jobs. We give council tax exemptions worth a total of £10m to 9000 households, hand out £1m a year in community grants, run free activities for families, support a network of food hubs, give advice on finances and debt, and so much more.

Since Labour came to power, we’ve halved the Council’s carbon emissions, we’ve planted over 4000 trees across the city, we’ve brought in new parks and nature reserves like Logan’s Meadow and Hobson’s Park. We’re investing in a solar farm to power our bin lorries, and a renewable energy district heat network to decarbonise public buildings.

I would love to have the opportunity to work for you in Petersfield, and for the whole of Cambridge as the City Council leader.

I am deeply connected to Cambridge; I was born here, studied here and now work here. Cambridge is one of the most unequal and nature-deprived places in the UK, and those things are intrinsically linked: we will not avoid climate collapse without reducing inequality.

As a councillor, I will prioritise the care of those who are most vulnerable, including our growing elderly population, the disabled community, and people who are homeless or in insecure housing. I will also be a voice for young people in Petersfield who are bearing the brunt of the mental health crisis due to climate anxiety and lack of amenity investment. I hope to put local people and our natural world back at the heart of our communal decision making.

I have long been passionate about equality and community support. During the pandemic, I volunteered for Cambridge Community Kitchen and Waterbeach Foodbank. I am a proud feminist, LGBTQAI+ ally and antiracist, and care greatly about accessibility for people with disabilities. Due to my background in architecture and landscape design I am well-versed in the policies that have led to the housing crisis, and what we can do about it. I’m in favour of more council housing and introducing rent controls so tenants can have greater stability.

profile.statement_to_voters:
If there is one positive the Coronavirus has taught us, it is this: caring, vibrant, local community life is essential; essential for our quality of life and, at times, essential for our very survival.

The importance of community was clear throughout my 45-year career in education, and it could not be clearer during this global epidemic.

I have lived in Brentwood for more than 33 years and the town has everything going for it.

There are parks, woodland and open spaces within minutes of the High Street. It has history as a route centre since the days of the Romans and the Pilgrims, and is within easy reach of London and the coast. Greens want to revive communities like ours. Give Greens the chance!

profile.statement_to_voters:
Iain Dickson (Gossops Green) is a long serving member of the Green Party and an even longer serving member of the Crawley community. Iain was also the Green Party prospective parliamentary candidate for Crawley and stood in the 2019 and 2025 General election gaining Crawley Green Party’s best ever result!

“I am standing as the Green Party candidate in Southgate and Gossops Green to help people and to improve our community by putting people and the environment first. As a councillor, I believe I can help and improve the area through better Council provision and community action.” - Iain Dickson

Zack Ali got married in 2009 and made Crawley his home. Since then, he has been a resident of Broadfield. Zack is a proud father of two young boys, both of whom went to Hilltop Primary School in Southgate.  He has received an honours degree in Business management and finance from University of Surrey.

In 2007 he worked for Domino’s Pizza and quickly ended up managing several stores across Sussex and Surrey. He was based at the store on Crawley High Street and led a team of store managers from across the region and was responsible for more than 200 staff. The Crawley store became one of the top 10 performing stores worldwide, whilst under Zack’s leadership. He went on to start his own business, this grew it to the point where it employed over 50 people and served thousands of customers every year.

Zack was elected to Crawley Borough Council and West Sussex County Council in 2021, representing Southgate and Gossops Green division of Crawley. He has sat on several committees and is a member of the shadow cabinet for Planning and Economic development on Crawley Borough Council.

He has been actively involved in Crawley politics since 2007, having worked alongside Henry Smith MP on various local issues and campaigns. Zack has also played a key role in several general election campaigns supporting Henry Smith MP to win the Crawley seat.

He has worked with a number local and national charities in support of a range charitable causes and issues. Including during the pandemic, where he gathered several likeminded people to set up a group to support vulnerable local residents. This group did grocery and food shopping for shielding families, vulnerable households and those on low incomes.

Zack also joined the NHS Volunteer Responders and helped local communities across Crawley to stay safe and feel supported. Taking part in activities such as welfare phone calls with residents living alone and dropping of medication and care packages.

My career has been in software engineering. I've worked in events media, in finance and currently in defence where I manage a project, architect the solutions and write the code. I've spent the last 20 years facing complex problems and solving them. I have been living in Crawley since 2016 and lived in Surrey before that.

I voted Leave in the 2016 referendum. I've never regretted that vote nor the following times we had to hammer the point home to our political class. Like so many others I voted for Boris in the hope that we had an optimistic, British patriot who would champion our people, our country and our values and reverse the downward trends we all experienced. Like so many others I have now seen what the Conservative party has become.

I will never give up on Britain, her values, people and history. With a strong reform of our institutions, we will save Britain.

My priorities are focussed on public safety, the safety of women and girls and to stop the rampant crime that is happening through Crawley.  Spending is out of control and that needs to be brought back to sensible levels and only on what benefits the good people of Crawley.  There are a myriad of other issues to resolve as well, such as restoring the high street, get council house repairs done as close to first time as possible, stop West of Ifield expansion and resist the illegal immigrant settlement into our communities putting our safety at great risk.  None of us at Reform are single issue, we hear all perspectives and record all problems raised to us for addressing.

Oh, and fix the damn potholes.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I’m Chris Halliday, a data analyst and local resident in Ashton upon Mersey, and I’m proud to be part of the Conservative team holding this failing Labour council to account.

Labour’s Ashton councillors are backing plans to build on Goodwood Field, against the wishes of local residents. I will oppose any plans to build on this vital green space.

Labour have lost control of Trafford’s finances, raising council tax by 7.5% two years in a row, above the referendum threshold, while bringing back green bin charges.
At the same time, we have some of the worst potholes in Greater Manchester, blocked drains and missed bins. We’ll address this decline with a clear plan to focus on the basics and get spending under control.

Trafford has fantastic schools, but too many children are being sent to places far away from where they live, putting parents and children under pressure. I’ll fight for a new secondary school in South Trafford and help families get their children into local schools, not miles away.

Only the Conservatives have a chance to get Labour out in Ashton and send a message to this failing council. If, like me, you want to see:

- Protection of our green spaces in Goodwood Field and Carrington Moss
- Council tax under control, not amongst the highest in the country
- Focusing on getting the basics right
- Enough local school places for local children

Vote for Chris Halliday and the Conservatives on May 7th.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I'm Daniel Davey, I was born and raised in Ipswich. I've had the town in my heart throughout my working career in the pub and leisure industry. In my 4 years mananging and running pubs & bars, I've seen and been friends with people from across the spectrum of industries and upbringings. With this experience the one thing I can tell you is finding the right education and mentors is the true key to success.

I developed my interest in politics during 2010, finding it fascinating that two parties came together for a more balanced government. Though on my journey I discovered many within the LibDems felt this as a betrayal within the party, as a new steward looking in, I would hate to think what might have happened if they had not tempered their partners in power at this critical juncture in British history, especially looking back now what has happened without that calming moral presence.

I now feel the world and more importantly, locally, that calming voice is needed again. We have become fractured. It's time to turn that tide back to unity and cooperation. Time to work for long term success instead of short-term one-upmanship that blight the larger parties. As the old adage goes, be the change you wish to see, that is why I am running and that is why I also think you should not ignore the local elections this year. Who you vote for on the 2nd May won't affect the Brexit mess but will affect what happens to your local community and to Ipswich in the coming years. One of the councillors' key  roles is being your first point of contact & to represent you if you have issues and concerns in your local community and I hope to be that voice and man of action for you in the Whitton Ward. Should you wish to contact me, please email me at info@ipswichlibdems.org.uk

question: Who will win the Didsbury East ward in the Manchester local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
I am very proud of what we say in our Lib Dem constitution: “The Liberal Democrats exist to build and safeguard a free, fair and open society, in which we seek to balance the fundamental values of liberty, equality and community, and in which no-one shall be enslaved by poverty, ignorance or conformity”.

At the age of 25 I was elected as the youngest councillor on Preston Borough Council serving for 8 years. I have represented the party in three general elections, and have been part of the Southport Lib Dem team since 2010.

Southport faces challenges and, like so many seaside towns throughout the country, needs more support from the government, and from the local council. My priorities would be to do everything I can to boost jobs and the local economy, to support our NHS and schools, to protect our environment, and to help those in Southport who face poverty, disadvantage, or other hurdles. Everyone should have a fair chance to get on in life.

I also think MPs should be more open, transparent, honest and accountable. They should be more accessible to the people who elect them. And they should put the people and the country’s interests before their own and their party’s. We deserve a much better calibre of MPs than many of those we’ve got at the moment; we need people with courage and integrity.

question: Who will win the Croston, Mawdesley & Euxton South ward in the Chorley local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
Sarah Haithcock lives in East Chesterton and works at a local special educational needs school. She has dedicated her professional life to service and wishes to bring that same commitment to the City Council as your representative.

As your councillor, she will continue to listen to your concerns, deliver practical solutions and make our area safer, greener and fairer.

Having spoken with hundreds of residents, her key priorities for East Chesterton are:

1. Building strong and resilient communities
She will work with the community and local authorities to tackle anti-social behaviour such as drug dealing, speeding, littering and fly tipping to make East Chesterton safe for everyone.

2. High quality homes for all residents
She wants to support the building of a new generation of environmentally friendly and accessible council homes. She will support residents in need of housing and work with community groups, authorities and charities to tackle rough sleeping. She is committed to protecting renters rights and improving access to affordable homes.

3. Cheaper, easier and safer travel
She will campaign for more frequent local buses, protected cycle lanes and safer pedestrian crossings.

Sarah has been active in the community beyond her professional life. She has listened to hundreds of residents over recent years and supported individuals with their specific issues.

Her work in the community includes:

- Leading police walkabouts to identify anti-social behaviour hotspots with local authorities
- Challenging the County Council on the dimming of streetlights across the city
- Campaigning against cuts to holiday food vouchers for the most vulnerable children in our community

Ania has lived in Cambridge since 2015. Having grown up in Poland, Ania is one of many scientists who have come to Cambridge for work and decided to make it their permanent home.

Having worked for a Cambridge-based company for the past 10 years, and now taking on a freelance role, Ania is keen to make sure that Cambridge works for everyone equitably – residents, businesses, visitors and nature.

She is also passionate about evidence-based medicine, and using best available data to make healthcare decisions.

Her love of dance led her to volunteer for a local dance society to increase access to affordable community social classes.

In her spare time, Ania is learning to play the trumpet and trains with a local club in Latin Formation dancing. Ania is also a keen cyclist and loves going on walks with her corgi.

If elected she will work with Bob Illingworth and Ian Manning to stand up for East Chesterton residents and make your voice heard.

question: Who will win the East Chesterton ward in the Cambridge local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
I live on Standfield, in the heart of Abbots Langley with my wife and children – it’s our home. So protecting our village isn’t a political slogan for me – it’s personal.

Although I voted Conservative, I’d never been a party member until February.  I’m a technical engineer, not a politician – I just want the best for our village and our children.

But our Green Belt is at risk like never before.  Distracted by the infighting that caused Sara Bedford to resign, Lib Dems missed the deadline to pass a Local Plan to avoid Labour trebling housebuilding targets.

Only Conservatives were focused on solving the problem. While Lib Dems surrendered to losing our Green Belt, Conservatives spent hundreds of hours forensically identifying solutions from councils around England.

And my neighbours and I worry that others are using this election as a political stepping stone: to score points, not solve problems.  I live locally, but that's not the case of the only candidates who put their address on the ballot paper - you can see for yourself that the Lib Dem candidate lives in Nash Mills, Hemel Hempstead and Reform in Chorleywood.

Electing Conservative Vicky Edwards last year has been a breath of fresh air. As a local dad and engineer, I just want to solve problems in place of Lib Dem infighting, and save our Green Belt.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Dear Woodbridge Neighbour, 
I have had the privilege of serving as your County Councillor for the past two and a half years, and it has been an honour to work on behalf of Woodbridge. During that time, I have focused on delivering practical improvements that make a real difference—from resurfacing the riverside pathway to tackling flooding on the A12, improving pavements in the Thoroughfare, and securing better bus services for residents and students.

I believe in not accepting “no” when there is a better outcome to be achieved. That determination is what drives me to keep pushing for results for our community.

Looking ahead, Woodbridge faces important challenges. We need strong, experienced representation to guide us through Local Government Reorganisation, to stand up for our town as major projects like Sizewell C place increasing pressure on our infrastructure, and to ensure schemes such as Active Travel and A12 improvements work in our best interests.

I will continue to be a clear voice for Woodbridge—championing safer crossings, better access, improved local facilities, and the protection of our environment.

I am committed to working hard, listening to residents, and delivering for our community.

Thank you

profile.statement_to_voters:
I’m proud to stand for Arborfield and Barkham ward, where I’m living for the last 6 years. A community where families, young people, and nature all deserve to thrive.

As someone passionate about protecting our environment and supporting future generations, I want to help create a safer, greener, and more accessible place for everyone, especially our pupils.

If elected as your Green Councillor, I will focus on:

**A greener future for our children:**
– Protecting local green spaces from overdevelopment
– Planting more trees and creating wildlife-friendly areas.
– Expanding no-mow verges and wildflower spaces near schools
– Reducing waste and boosting recycling across the ward

**Safer streets for pupils and families:**
– Introducing 20mph zones around schools and residential areas
– Creating safer crossings and better walking routes to school
– Supporting traffic-free or low-traffic zones at school drop-off times
– Tackling air pollution to ensure cleaner, healthier journeys

**Better, accessible transport:**
– Improving cycle lanes and safe bike storage for students
– Pushing for more reliable and affordable public transport
– Expanding electric vehicle charging while prioritising active travel

**Supporting wellbeing and opportunity:**
– Championing mental health support for young people
– Ensuring safe, inclusive community spaces for all ages
– Advocating for fair funding for local services and schools

Together, we can build an Arborfield and Barkham that puts people, planet, and future generations first.

I'm a dedicated Year 6 primary school teacher and a community event organiser, standing to represent Barkham and Arborfield. Through my work in education I support children and families every day, using strong listening, leadership, and problem-solving skills. I also organise Indian cultural events that bring residents together and strengthen community bonds. I am passionate about education, inclusion, and strong local services, and I am committed to serving residents and helping our community remain welcoming, safe, and connected.

profile.statement_to_voters: Helen (Ellie) Austen has lived and worked in Broxbourne for more than 20 years.  She is an active volunteer with two community garden schemes in the Broxbourne area.  As a teacher she is concerned with greening the curriculum.  She is also working to improve access to an appropriate school place for all members of our local population.

I’ve been proud to call Cheshunt home for over nine years. In that time, I’ve witnessed how much our community has changed, sometimes for the better, but all too often, vital services have been stretched thin while local voices have gone unheard. I’ve always had a deep interest in politics, but it was the last general election that inspired me to take a more active role in local political life. I believe that change begins at the community level, and that we each have a responsibility to speak up and get involved in shaping a better future for everyone.

One of my core priorities is working with local organisations to end violence against women and girls. Broxbourne must be a place where women feel safe, supported, and heard. That means creating a dedicated safe haven for domestic abuse victims, strengthening the laws that protect them, and properly funding the support services they rely on. But just as crucially, we must invest in education — in our schools, homes, and wider community — to break the cycle of abuse before it begins. Every woman deserves safety, justice, and respect. By coming together as a community, we can build a Broxbourne where those values are more than just words — they’re a lived reality.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Thank you for taking the time to Look at my profile. My name is Martin power and I'm a member of United Kingdom Independence party I joined the party 2009 I was inspired by a conference that I attended at Torquay. I had decided to look at different political parties as I became very this appointed with the main parties I felt the didn't represent me.

The United Kingdom Independence party reminded me of what democracy is I was never comfortable been a member of the European Union. I was unhappy that on the elected politicians had a huge say in my day-to-day life our laws our courts. I decided that I too must get involved with UKIP to break away from the European Union and happily I was every part of that great event that did take place.

UKIP has evolved into a political force in British politics UKIP is not afraid to challenge the bigger parties on policies regarding   bettering our lives here in Britain. I feel it's a real privilege to be able to fight an election in 2017 as we are the only party that can make sure we do leave the European Union. We believe in an English parliament for English MPs to make decisions about England. We want to abolished a House of Lords as it is old antiquated and serves no real purpose in British democracy. We want to take control of our borders while we welcome emigration we want to be able to choose who comes into our country.We want to be rebuilt our army,  navy and air force, put more police on the streets, extra border patrol officers and extra prison officers. We want to abolish the Barnett formula where Scottish people get twice as much money per head from England than do English people. We want to make apprenticeships in skills just as important as University degrees. We would like to take back  10 billion foreign aid as we believe charity begins at home and we have an awful lot of problems in this country that needs to be addressed one been the overburdened NHS. We would continue to contribute to foreign aid and having taken the 10 billion away we would still be contributing the same foreign aid as America. We have a great manifesto please Google UKIP 2017 manifesto and read as we have lots of policies as we are a political party and not as some would say a pressure group.
Thank you for taking the time to read this statement and that as you do you should think about voting for a United Kingdom Independence party MP as we are the only party that can guarantee and hold the government to account on Europe.
Thank you Martin power candidate for United Kingdom Independence party Blackley and Broughton

Hello,
My name is Dean Luke Booth and I am an Independent Candidate for Higher Blackley in the upcoming local election which will take place on the Thursday 7th May. A local election is simply an opportunity to choose the best candidate to represent your community and has little impact on central government. As an Independent I am not told how to vote by any political party so my focus will remain solely on improving Higher Blackley, supporting our community, acting on your behalf and fighting for the things that matter to you.
I have been a Higher Blackley resident my entire life so I am well aware of the issues we face. 
𝗠𝘆 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀
👮 To work with our local policing team to reduce crime and ensure 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲𝘆 is a safe & enjoyable place to live and work.
🍃 To ensure environmental issues are dealt with promptly, whilst protecting our parks & green spaces.
🫁 To promote health & wellbeing in 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲𝘆 through addiction dependency programs & community outreach projects.
🗣️ To communicate with residents on a regular basis to ensure they are aware of decisions, events and support available to them.
👂 To listen and act upon the needs of residents, community groups and businesses in 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲𝘆.
🚌 Fighting for much needed additional bus routes to help residents get to appointments, supermarkets like Aldi or the gym.
⚽️ Utilise community buildings in Higher Blackley to support the community and our children.
𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗜 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗽 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵:
❇️ Pot Holes
❇️ Fly Tipping
❇️ Blocked Drains
❇️ Environmental Issues
❇️ Pest Control
❇️ Road Markings
❇️ Road Signs
❇️ Street Lighting
❇️ Financial Advice
❇️ Housing Advice
❇️ Employment Advice
❇️ Bins & Waste Removal Services

You can follow my 𝗙𝗮𝗰𝗲𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗸 page for updates, events, advice etc - www.facebook.com/deanlukeboothHB. Email me deanlukebooth@gmail.com or call / text / WhatsApp me on 07999 437255.

I’d love the opportunity to work on your behalf. Is there anything I can do for you? Can I count on your vote on the 7th May?

I look forward to hearing from you.

Yours faithfully,
Dean Luke Booth.

I grew up in Blackpool and have seen the effects of austerity first-hand. Like Blackpool, Higher Blackley has been abandoned by Westminster and taken for granted by successive governments. Since moving to Higher Blackley, I have felt an instant connection with the community and want to be a champion for its diverse constituency.

I’m proud to stand for the Green Party, representing those too often ignored or scapegoated by other parties. Too many young people are being left without clear pathways forward, we need to invest in opportunities and positive futures for young people in our community.

3. Improving road safety and the state of rural roads.

4. Fighting for better facilities for our young people.

Kay says that she will serve the residents of the division to the best of her ability and make sure that she is always available to hear ideas and concerns.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I work as an Energy Specialist at Octopus Energy, focusing on smart tariffs (electric vehicles, solar panels, heat pumps etc) and helping people with their bills.

I’m decided to put my name forward to stand in Radcliffe West for the Greens because I believe local politics should be about fairness, equality, and genuinely representing everyone in the community. I love that the Green Party stands for inclusion, respect, and giving people a real voice, and that’s the approach I want to bring as a councillor.

My main priority, if elected, would be standing up for equality in the ward and pushing back against the increasingly divisive tone in local politics. I’m strongly against the kind of conversations and symbolism that try to split communities apart — including the recent topical focus on “us vs them” messaging. profile.statement_to_voters:
You are choosing a new local councillor in the l upcoming local council by-election on Thursday 5th March.

We are being let down by our existing local councillors, they are saying one thing and then doing the opposite.  Whether it's Labour councillors promising to not cut free public events, and 
then cancelling the fireworks. Or Reform Councillors saying they'll cut your Council Tax and then raising it. You need a better Councillor.

I'm a hardworking local man who like many have been struggling. I work full time for the NHS and I deeply care about people. I'm a former councillor who knows how to get things done and have a strong  track record of positive change for my area, including £100,000's of investment into the roads and hundreds of fixed potholes, new and improved play areas and local community groups receing funding in the tens of thousands. That is what I bring as your candidate and what I want to do for Spital.
The upcoming local council by-election is a chance to vote for change.
A  vote for me is a vote to invigorate our town centre and support small businesses. A vote for me is a vote to support local households who need help from the council, not council tax rises. A vote for me is a way to get a community champion elected to the council who is focused on tackling  issues like potholes, over development and traffic.

These are the issues I will focus on. What I offer is experience at scrutinising council decisions and uncovering hidden issues that will affect us all. I want 
to stop things like wasting public money and hiding decisions away from the public.

I will shine a light on the decisions being made that are hidden from you,  like Reforms County Councillors delaying flood prevention strategies and 
local road repairs and Labour Borough Councillors cutting more free town centre events.

This election will not change the political control of Tamworth Borough 
Council, but it is a chance to shine a light on what is happening at the 
council. 
Some candidates will promise you things they can’t deliver, or things that 
are nothing to do with the council. 
I promise you only what I can deliver. 
A vote for me in the upcoming by-election is a vote for someone who will 
act on local issues and be your community champion.

I will work hard for you both in the Council Chamber and in our 
neighbourhood. Including working with current councillors, police and community groups to form a community surgery.

Thank you for taking the time to hear from me and if you want to get in 
touch with me, please do drop me an email on 
brett@localconservatives.org.uk.

I am Stephen Andrews, and I am standing as your local Green Party candidate for Spital Ward.

This by-election comes at a crucial time for Tamworth; we are about to see the largest change in how our council is run and how services are delivered in a generation. At a moment like this, Spital needs a strong, local voice that listens to residents, stands up for the community, and puts people first.

For too long, residents have been let down by the two-party system. The Conservatives and Labour have both failed to deliver for Tamworth. Reform are no different: they have no policies for Spital and no plan for Tamworth. I believe that we deserve better. We deserve honest representation that puts Tamworth first and prioritises our future.

I look at Spital and the failure of the past few years is evident:
•	You see the pain that leaseholders have on your everyday lives. 
•	You see the failure of the Government to hold water companies to account for pollution in our rivers and crumbling infrastructure, leading to floods that we’re all too familiar with.
•	You see the potential negative implications of new developments being built.

Instead, I want to be the voice to work towards change:
•	I will advocate for the end of the leasehold system; and work with residents who are struggling with getting essential repairs done.
•	I will put pressure on the water companies, making sure they are held accountable.
•	I will research, question, and challenge all new developments, ensuring the town’s best interests are at heart.

I am ready to put in the work to ensure that every voice is heard; together, we can make this happen.

profile.statement_to_voters: Liz has lived in Charlbury for 30 years.  And been a town councillor for twelve of those (plus the 2020 ‘bonus’ year). When Charlbury town council was petitioned to declare a climate emergency - and did so unanimously in November 2019 -She was asked to help explore with residents what that might mean.  She worked with councillors and residents to set up a working group of the council, catchily titled ‘Environment Working Group’. Liz was pleased when WODC declared a climate emergency and she applauds its moves to plan for delivering on it.    If elected, she would take a keen interest in modifying planning policies to create a zero carbon buildings stock as effectively and quickly as possible; and to transform the district’s journeys infrastructure to promote public transport and pedestrian- and cycle-friendly networks.

Dear Neighbour,

Labour and Green supporters know this will be a two horse race so I'm very grateful to those who have already told me they will lend me their vote this time.

Jan Lund
Shipton resident and Lib Dem Candidate for Ascott & Shipton ward.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Chris Siddall has lived in the Halstead Division for more than 35 years with his wife and two children. He is the Managing Director of his own 45 year old company Eastern Plastics Machinery Ltd based in Earls Colne. His work involves helping plastic moulding companies develop better manufacturing systems that minimise waste, reduce the use of plastics, save energy and improve recycling. Chris is a specialist in JIT (Just In Time manufacturing) he has worked extensively throughout the UK but also all over the world with companies such as Johnston & Johnson, Gillette, Plessey to name a few.

He was Chairman of Governors at the ‘Earls Colne Primary School and Nursery’ for five years, is a trustee on the ‘Halstead Educational Trust’, helping give young people the opportunity to excel in education and life.

Chris served as a Braintree District Councillor for The Colnes, first elected in 2008, during his time at Braintree District Council he held various positions: Cabinet Member for Performance and Efficiency, Cabinet Member for Prosperity and Growth and Deputy Leader.

Over the years he has worked hard for the local area and served on various organisations as a non-remunerated director, ‘Ignite Business’ a business development company in Braintree helping start-up companies become established and existing companies grow, ‘The Warner Archive’ and the ‘Braintree Museum’ helping to preserve the historical heritage of our area.

Chris Siddall is a hard working local family man who is committed to vigorously representing the best interests of the people of the Halstead Division “What effects you effects me, I know how to get things done, if elected to Essex County Council I would work my socks off to get your voice heard”.

I have lived and worked in Halstead for 20 years,
and I have been a Green Party member since 2014.

As a resident, I see the strain our town is under: over-development without infrastructure, the decline of our roads and public transport, constant cuts to vital assets such as our schools and library, and an ever present threat to our green spaces.

The Green Party have a proven track record of getting things done, with district and county councillors in Essex, including here in Braintree District.
We work hard to make a difference in local communities.

As a new Green Party candidate, I may not have the public profile of some of the
others standing in this election but please do not doubt that, if elected, I will work hard for all residents.

If elected, I will be a strong advocate for Halstead at County Hall and will represent
everyone regardless of their age, socio-economic group, nationality, religion, race,
gender, sexuality, and even their politics.
 
I am standing in this election to offer a bright future for Halstead based on positivity, hope and community.

question: Who will win the Birchwood ward in the City of Lincoln local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
Katie has lived in Market Ward, near the Grafton centre, for nearly 18 years and has been your city councillor since 2019.  She has worked in Higher Education in Student Services for three decades and until very recently was employed at Anglia Ruskin University for more than 17 years as a Student Adviser, dealing with students in distress or with ongoing health conditions to help them succeed in their studies.

She is also a parent to a teenager and was a regular volunteer at the School Streets barrier at St Matthew's school whilst her child was at school there.

She is passionate about local democracy and is proud to have worked with fellow Lib Dem councillors to have successfully reduced single use plastics on our open spaces and stopped herbicide use on our green spaces. She fights on for better basic services in the city centre and around Cambridge - particularly public toilets. She has worked hard to ensure that events taking place on our public open spaces reduce their impact on the grass, arguing for proper funding from event organisers to repair and upgrade the soil and to return spaces to public use as soon as possible.

During her time as your councillor in Market ward, she campaigned successfully to keep the cows on our commons to increase biodiversity, to save trees in the ward from felling, and as our Planning and Transport and previously Housing spokesperson, has worked hard to improve housing around the city, including desperately needed affordable homes and the provision of decent timely infrastructure like schools and GP surgeries.

Katie has been a long standing advocate for the opportunity for part time and flexible working for all who want it, as well as for the wider Equalities agenda to encourage more diversity in the workplace and in politics. She understands that for many issues affecting our wards, local politics is about getting things changed for the better and working collaboratively with whoever can help to achieve this. It is often these changes that make a huge difference to our quality of life, whether that is updating parking restrictions to free up pavement space and make us safer, keeping residents informed and included on forthcoming developments like the Grafton Centre, or reporting potholes, graffiti or broken lampposts.

Many of you will see Katie regularly as she is out and about around the ward and please do stop and say hello and let her know if there is anything that she and the wider Market Lib Dem of Tim, Anthony and Alex can help you with.

I moved to Cambridge to begin my studies in biomedical sciences and quickly fell in love with the community, town and history of Cambridge.

With so much turbulence and negative media around it’s now more important than ever that we band together : from the smallest community, to our national identity. I want to ensure we are creating a future where people are the beating heart of our community and city.

I have a broad experience including training fire fighters, as a field medic and in the biomedical world. I am standing to help make an environment where all types of people feel listened to, supported and heard. From making more spaces for minorities to come together and thrive, to bringing groups together in celebrating the diversity of our city.

Hi, I’m standing in Market ward because I think the council has drifted away from everyday concerns.
Too many decisions are being made without thinking through the real-world impact—on housing, on small businesses, and on how people actually get around the city.
I support Reform UK because they’re pushing for practical, common-sense policies—lower costs, better value for taxpayers, and more accountability. Locally, it’s about making sure Cambridge works for the people who live and work here.
That means better scrutiny of developments, supporting local businesses, and making sure policies don’t make everyday life harder or more expensive.
I want to bring a bit more balance and realism back into how decisions are made here in Cambridge.

Although born in Lancashire, John moved to East Suffolk before his second birthday.
