question: Who will win the Wellington ward in the Rushmoor local election on 2026-05-07?

profile.statement_to_voters:
I joined the Green Party 12 years ago to try to address unfairness, inequality and the climate emergency. I am a retired Local Authority senior manager and have had several roles in the Party, including Ward Organiser and as a member of the Executive.

I’m standing in this election as I am seeking to join other Green councillors in implementing sustainable policies for Sheffield. I support plans for Active Travel and a reduction in air pollution. I have a disabled husband, so I am very aware that any developments need to include consultation with and provision for disabled people. Other priorities are to increase recycling, to improve access to effective building insulation, renewable energy and other measures to reduce the use of fossil fuels.

If elected as your local Councillor, I would listen to and work on behalf of all residents across all areas of the Dore and Totley ward.

I’m a local business owner, and I have lived within 2 miles of Dore and Totley my whole life and a proud father and grandfather who cares deeply about the future of our community. Having lived and worked here in Sheffield for decades, I’ve seen first-hand how decisions made at the Town Hall affect everyday life — and right now, too many of those decisions are failing local people.

Sheffield is facing a serious challenge. The council is over £1.2 billion in debt, with hundreds of thousands of pounds being spent every week just on interest. That’s money not being used to fix our roads, improve services, or support residents — it’s simply paying for past mismanagement. Meanwhile, our streets are crumbling, services are declining, and residents are being asked to pay the price.

This isn’t sustainable — and it isn’t acceptable.

Across the city, people can see things aren’t working. Expensive schemes like so-called “Dutch-style” roundabouts are confusing and ineffective. Traffic management systems have made congestion worse, not better, leaving drivers stuck in queues day after day. At the same time, parking charges in the city centre have become so high that they are driving people away — hurting local businesses and contributing to boarded-up shops and declining footfall.

We’ve also seen poorly designed cycle lanes that are rarely used yet have reduced road capacity and added to congestion. Recycling centres are often miles away, difficult to access, and operate limited hours — making it harder, not easier, for residents to do the right thing.

The list goes on — and it all points to the same problem: decisions being made without proper thought for the people who live and work here.

That’s why I’m standing in the council election: to bring accountability, common sense, and real delivery back into local government.

A key priority for me is fixing our roads properly and promptly. Residents are fed up with potholes, broken surfaces, and repeated short-term fixes that don’t last. I will push for contractors to be held accountable and for proper, long-term repairs that deliver value for taxpayers.

I am also committed to protecting our green belt and countryside. I will oppose inappropriate development that threatens our environment and village identity, while ensuring that planning decisions truly reflect the wishes of local communities.

Our local economy and community life must be supported and protected. Small businesses, pubs, and shops are under increasing pressure from tax and wage increases not to mention the sourcing costs of delivering their products and service to us all. yet they are vital to the character and strength of our area, and must be protected and supported.

Above all, I believe Sheffield needs a council that serves residents, focuses on delivery, and spends public money wisely. That means tackling waste, improving efficiency, and making sure every pound works for the people of Sheffield — not against them.

I will be a councillor who listens, acts, and delivers — putting residents first, protecting our area, and restoring pride in our community.

Hi, I’m Nicola Smith, your candidate for Labour in Dore & Totley Ward.
If you choose to vote for me, I promise to do my best and will work hard. I am honest and have a wide variety of experience which I will use to ensure our ward gets the very best representation.
I founded a successful tech business which has been running 8 years, real proof I understand some of the difficulties affecting local businesses. I am also someone that moves with the times and know when modern technology and how it can be utilised for good.
As a school governor at Totley Primary School, I’ve learnt a huge amount about our education system and our local schools. I have pushed forward the healthy eating and have been the link governor for the community, early years, and PSHE.
I've been campaigning and making real change all my life. My first successful campaign age 12 saw my school uniform change. I founded a charity week that has ran 6 times and spread to 3 continents. It was mentioned in the House of Lord. Locally I campaigned to slow down the traffic on my road, which is now a 20-mph limit and for more blue bin space, which is happening in the coming months. I have bags of energy and often find if you push from many angles, if the change is needed the door it happens. 
As a proud Yorkshire woman, passionate about building strong, connected communities. I’ve lived in Totley with my husband and daughters.
From the moment we arrived, we knew we’d made the right choice to live here. Our ward has an incredible sense of community, beautiful landscapes on our doorstep, and fantastic local schools where our daughters now learn and grow. Being part of this community has inspired me every day to help make our neighbourhood stronger, more inclusive, and supportive for everyone who calls it home.
I’m actively involved in local life: As well as serving as a school governor, I manage a Porter Girls football team and am a leader at Beaver Scouts. You’ll see me manning stalls at local events, joining local litter picks, and tree planning sessions and doing anything I can for our community. I previously acted as a trustee at Wiz Kids Nursery and during the COVID-19 pandemic, I delivered medication to vulnerable people in our area. These experiences have given me a deep understanding of the needs and hopes of our community, and I am committed to using that insight to make a positive difference where it matters most.
Professionally, I ran a successful tech business with my husband for the last 7 years and have recently started working full time doing marketing. Additionally, I officiate funerals, which is truly an honour to celebrate someone’s life.

I may be younger than the other candidates, however I have a wealth of life experience. Here’s a few things I’m proud of. I’ve lived and worked on 4 continents, I’ve volunteered and been a Trustee of a Yorkshire Samaritan’s branch, I’ve worked as a career for both older people and people with additional needs. I’ve got a degree in genetics and was President of an international Human Rights organisation representing 40,000 members.

If you’d like to tell me about your local concerns or hear more, I’d love to hear from you.
And finally, if you want someone that truly cares, will listen and has a proven track record of getting things done, vote me on 7th May.

Tracey was born in Urmston and has lived in Trafford most of her life. In the autumn of 2021 she set up the Petition “no cones on Chester Road” in a direct response to resident’s frustrations caused by increased congestion and pollution in close proximity to 4 primary schools. At the time of presentation to Trafford Council, it had achieved 2,742 signatures and media coverage. Tracey felt like it was a lonely crusade to be honest through the minefield of Labour opposition yet the Conservatives cared and this experience inspired Tracey to “cross the floor” and stand as an election candidate in a hope to continue to represent residents.

Tracey has a plethora of life and work experiences. Working in the hospitality industry led to joint business’ enterprises in a catering company and cafe in Trafford, whilst simultaneously completing 2 house renovations.

Then, as a single parent to 4 young children she achieved a Bachelor of Social Science 2:1 with Honours at Manchester University, prior to a successful career as a Retail Store Manager with the Cooperative.

Tracey has in recent years, cared for young adults with mental health issues and the elderly, alongside a part time office administration role supporting her husband’s Legal business.

In addition to which, Tracey has actively assisted a local community group to “green the alleyways” securing a £6,000 grant from Groundworks.

Life for Tracey has been a bit of a rollercoaster and she expects that to continue in our current uncertain climate. Tracey sincerely wishes to aid residents in their ups and downs too.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I am your Bury Independent candidate for Elton ward in Bury Council’s local elections due to take place on Thursday 2nd May 2024.

I am standing as the Bury Independent candidate for Elton ward in the May elections because I feel that the needs of local people will be better served by a locally based group rather than by the main national political parties. Our aim is to get the best deal for the people of Elton and Bury generally and, above all, to listen to the real views of local people.

I am standing as a councillor for Elton ward because:

Bury has been failed by the two-party system with party issues often coming before local ones.

I believe in people before politics, listening first then acting.

Local people’s views should carry most weight in major decisions.

We need to take positive action on repairing our potholes and making our roads fit for purpose.

We need to stamp out fly tipping, a horrible blot on our landscape.

We need to look again at large building developments that have a huge impact on our environment.

We need to ensure that we have the infrastructure in place to support any future developments.

I won’t make any lavish promises as to what I can do if I get elected. The one promise I can make, and keep, is to do my absolute best for the people of Elton

I've lived in Bury since I was born, at Fairfield Hospital in 1999, and I went to school first at Holcombe Brook Primary School, then Woodhey High School and college at Holy Cross in Bury. Whilst many of my friends chose to go to university to study further, I chose to apply for an apprenticeship, and have been lucky to work for two Top 50 law firms whilst I worked and studied for my qualification.

This means I've been able to stay in the area my whole life, and I am very happy to call it home. I'm proud of where we live, and have for years enjoyed walking on the Kirklees Trail and in Burrs Country Park. There is nothing more rewarding than looking after our area and I have been pleased to do many litterpicks and work with local community groups to help. By going one step further and standing as a candidate, I am hoping that I will now have the chance to give more back to Bury, to Elton and to you.

The Elton Reservoir is our largest area of water in Bury and of great beauty for our plants and other wild life. We need to challenge our council to stop the building of more houses on the green belt.

Our roads aren't fit for purpose, not just the potholes, but the lack of cycle ways and discouraging fly-tipping: we need to challenge our council on this area that needs developing.

Parents of children with additional leaning and communication needs feel let down by the council for not providing appropriate and effective support, both in the classroom and in the community. I know as I was a headteacher in a special school for over 30 years.

Our council does little for our teenagers in the community, with few options to thrive in designated centres. I give music sessions in our local care homes and hospitals voluntarily and have become aware how little activities exist in the community for our ageing population.

A review of our council's highest paid managers is required, as Bury's finances are in a mess, even though council taxes are at their highest ever!

Isabel Harrison believes in fairness and equality of opportunity for all, with people’s issues being supported through local government, not through the central state. Bedhampton deserves practical leadership, common sense and a councillor who will always put local people first. I want to make a positive difference.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I am a Chartered Accountant and a former president of the local branch of the Institute of Chartered Accountants (ICAEW). I am also a Chartered Tax Adviser and a former chair of the local branch of the Chartered Institute of Taxation (CIOT).
From 2022 to 2025 I was chair of the Milton Keynes Liberal Democrats.

I have lived in Milton Keynes since 1996, working as a tax adviser, and lately as a tax journalist. I am now retired and spend nearly all of my time working to get good people elected to local or national government.

Your local candidate. I live in Hanslope and have been it's City Councillor for 3 years. I've also been a Parish Councillor and held positions on the Rural Police & Crime Forum, Vice-Chair of Housing, Health and Adult Social Care Committee and other voluntary bodies.

I love where I live, love working here, and working hard. I've been working hard to improve our roads and pavements. Stop bad planning applications. And now working on making sure that the MK North proposed expansion does not go ahead.

I am a business women with a wide range of experience - insurance, motorsport, car industry, tech and more. I'm proud to call Milton Keynes home.

Our group has a manifesto - an actual plan for Milton Keynes - how to fix the basics and plan for the future.

I have my own pledges on my leaflets and you can see the things I've been working on and what I'm focussing on - detailed on my Facebook, Insta pages. And I'm availalbe by phone and email - hello@alison-andrew.co.uk or 09108 738777.

I am committed to improving and protecting our incredible Ward. You'll find me at many local events and always happy to meet for a beer or coffee!

After 35 years as a resident in the area running a farm and previous career as a Chartered Accountant, if elected, I’ll bring real world experience to serve our community of Hanslope.

A former lifelong Conservative voter I joined Reform in 2024 to drive the change Britain needs to work to improve our community.

Timothy Suffolk has lived in Cosham for many years.

profile.statement_to_voters: Rosemary Morlin has lived in Northfield Brook since 2001 and is a freelance editor. She has been active in the Leys Residents’ Association and has sat on Blackbird Leys Parish Council as well as being the founder and co-ordinator of the Mole Place Neighbourhood Watch.

My name is Paul Sims and I live in Greater Leys. I have lived in Greater Leys for almost 23 years now, having been a resident since the estate was built in 1995. I chose to live in Oxford as it is a place I have always loved, having first come here in the early 1980s. I have worked in the entertainment industry all my life and I love it, its likely many have seen me in and about Oxford at various venues either DJing or running Karaoke nights. My wife was born and raised in Oxfordshire and is supportive of my wish to become a councilor so as to be able to help our local community.

Labour see Blackbird Leys as a safe seat and have done little for the community. Show them Blackbird Leys matters and vote for Paul Sims. Someone who will champion local issues and causes.

MAKE YOUR VOTE COUNT.

Bullet Points:

1. Ensuring Roads Are Repaired And Maintained.
We all know how bad the roads get in Blackbird Leys. I will push for action to be taken to repair and maintain the roads making them safe for drivers and cyclists.

2. Tackling Crime.
I know people are concerned about drug dealing in areas where there are children. I am against vigilanty approaches to this as I think it makes the situation more confrontational. I will however work with the police to make areas such as parks, schools, community centers and shopping areas no go places for drug dealers. That way parents can feel safe letting their children go to such places. I will also work with the police to help deal with other crimes as well.

3.Anti Social Behavior.
Anti social behavior affects everyone and can ruin communities. I want to help tackle this within the council and also with the local police. I want to make people responsible for their actions. Oxford has a housing crisis and if people want to be anti social then I believe they do not want to be part of the community. There are plenty of people wanting homes who would appreciate having a home and not be anti social. I feel anti social behavior is one of the major issues of our time and should be dealt with firmly so that it is clear it is not acceptable.

4.Dealing With Parking Issues.
We all know parking is an issue in Blackbird Leys. I would like to see more off road parking spaces built for residents and their guests. I would also like to see much tougher action on illegal parking so as to free up roads. Currently parking enforcement seems to be non existent in Blackbird Leys and i would like to change that for the benefit of the whole community.

5. Unscrupulous Landlords.
I will work with others within the council to bring homes up to a decent living standard. Landlords who do not keep homes in a safe condition and exploit tenants will be held to account so that people have homes they can live in safely.

I am committed to building a stronger Leys community by working hard for better public services and opportunities for all residents.

The best thing about growing up on the Leys are the people.  I grew up on the Blackbird Leys and went to school in Redefield.  My friends and family live on the estate.

I was a youth worker at the Blackbird Leys youth centre for many years and enjoy bringing people together through music.  I have seen neighbours come together to support families in need, and young people express their creativity through music events at the youth centre and venues.

In the 1990s I organised local musicians and volunteers, secured funding, resulting in over £5,000 raised which established a live music project that ran for many years and I wrote a music proposal which later evolved in  Soundwork Studios providing ongoing music education and creative opportunities for hundreds of young people in our community. I feel passionately that our young people should have even more opportunities – not just in music but good jobs, homes and a future.

There are generations of people who make the Leys the good place that it is to live.  My vision is to build on the compassion and inclusiveness of Blackbird Leys by working hard to improve public services for everyone and a vibrant new community centre.

Having grown up on the estate and worked as a youth worker at Blackbird Leys youth centre, I have seen firsthand the talent and potential in our community. That’s why Ibelieve we need better housing, roads, jobs, and healthcare. As your representative, I will ensure residents’ voices are heard at City Council and that residents get the public services they deserve

Join me in building a brighter future for the Leys – where every resident has access to quality housing, jobs, healthcare, and opportunities to thrive.

David Newman (he/him) lives in Greater Leys, where he is a Blackbird Leys parish councillor and chair of the group preparing a neighbourhood plan.

He is keen to see the Leys area develop, and passionate about protecting our great green spaces and fighting the cost of living crisis by reducing energy costs.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Martin McCabe was born and raised in Worthing, Martin's goals are simple: to empower our community and demand a better politics that puts people first. A Building Engineer and lecturer, Martin is obsessed with making sure that politicians get the details right.

Martin has a strong record of standing up for local people. In 2018, he helped a local special needs charity find a new home so its work could continue. He has raised funds for local disability and women's football teams and campaigned for a People's Vote on Brexit. In May 2019, he is running the 100km London to Brighton ultra marathon to raise money for West Sussex Mind. Martin is the Chairman of the Adur and Worthing Liberal Democrats and our candidate for Tarring ward in the 2019 Worthing Council Elections.

Having spent many years working in the private sector, including senior management roles I bring a strong track record of leadership, accountability, and delivery. 
I care deeply about our local community and bring energy, enthusiasm, and a proactive approach to delivering the real change residents are calling for. People deserve a council that listens, takes action, and puts local priorities first.
My top priorities are:
•	Revitalising the town centre, celebrating local heritage, and protecting small businesses
•	Improving the condition of our roads
•	Tackling crime, antisocial behaviour, and shoplifting
•	Protecting our coastline and safeguarding green spaces
•	Managing major projects responsibly and ensuring value for money in council spending

Only Reform UK is offering the bold change needed locally. 
Labour tried to take away your democratic right to vote - Lend me your vote to make real positive changes for all of Worthing.

I have been living in West Worthing for eight years and am passionate about the local area and what it has to offer the local residents – if only it was cared for by the people in charge of building its fairer communities, keeping its water supplies clean, fixing the potholes, and keeping Worthing a safe town for future generations to grow up in. As someone who has two jobs and still worries about paying the bills on time every month, I know the challenges and worries of everyday life and will work my hardest to keep this town special and help it grow.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Hello residents I'm David Evans, your Reform UK Leigh West candidate. I am 44 years old, married with four children and we've lived in Leigh for over 30 years.

After seeing the deterioration of Leigh and the surrounding areas over 15 years, I became involved in local Politics.

In Leigh West, there are many issues, such as HMOs, poor road conditions and lack of infrastructure.

If elected, I will champion Leigh West and fight for

● No More HMOs
We need quality self contained apartments and housing, HMOs are not the answer.

● Improved Highways 
I will focus mainly on repair times for potholes and damaged street furniture

● Increased Public Funding
By cutting wasteful expenditure, we can re-invest into areas such as childrens play areas and cherished green spaces.

● Halt the sale of Council Land
Wigan Council should be 
utilizing this land for affordable and social housing, not selling it off for a quick profit.

Vote David Evans on May 7th

profile.statement_to_voters:
I’m Stephen Claffey, and I’m standing to be your Liberal Democrat councillor for Great Paxton Ward.

I live in Great Paxton with my family, and I care deeply about protecting village life across our ward, including Great Paxton, Great Gransden, Abbotsley, Waresley, Yelling and Toseland.

I’m not standing to play political games. I’m standing because too many local issues get left to drift, and people are fed up with not knowing who is responsible, what is being done, or whether anyone is properly listening.

As a parish councillor, I’ve already been involved in the kind of practical local work that matters to people, backing road safety improvements, supporting the village shop, helping push local issues forward, and working with residents and local bodies to get things moving.

That is how I believe local politics should work. Listen properly. Be visible. Understand the details. Work hard. Follow through.

If elected to Huntingdonshire District Council, I will focus on the things a good local councillor should focus on: strong representation, clear communication, protecting the character of our villages, supporting local services, and making sure rural communities are not overlooked.

I believe our area deserves a councillor who is rooted here, takes people seriously, and gets stuck into the issues that affect daily life.

If that is the kind of representation you want, I’d be grateful for your support.

I have made Abbotsley my home since 2014, having previously lived in London working as a solicitor. I bring the real-world experience, practical approach, and competence that we sorely need right now.

This is my first time standing for election, and I want to offer a straightforward, practical approach - focused on listening to residents, representing the villages properly, and getting things done.

As a resident of Abbotsley, and having previously served as President of Abbotsley WI, I care deeply about our communities and the people who live here.

I believe in financial prudence, fairness, and protecting the things that make our villages good places to live. Where something needs to be challenged, I will do so - but always with a focus on residents rather than rhetoric.

**After many years of declining services, it can sometimes feel as though little will change for the better. I believe that we can do better, by focusing on local priorities and amplifying residents' voices.**

profile.statement_to_voters: I have lived in Eastleigh for over 35 years and currently have run my shop in Eastleigh High Street for over 5 years. I’ve seen our community struggle while the Council wastes money and ignores local people. I’m standing to fight for our small businesses and comunity, tackle antisocial behaviour, and protect our green spaces from overdevelopment

I have lived in Eastleigh for the past decade and am proud to call it home. I’m standing in this by-election because I believe our town deserves better. After 30 years of Liberal Democrat control at Eastleigh Borough Council, too many people feel things are going backwards. It’s time for a fresh start.

If elected, I will focus on the basics: fixing our roads, tackling antisocial behaviour, revitalising the town centre, protecting and investing in our green spaces, and fighting cuts to local bus services. I will push for stronger action on street cleaning, better support for independent businesses, and a real effort to bring pride back to our high street.

I also support Labour’s plans to reform local government. Replacing the borough and county council structures with a single unitary authority and a directly elected mayor would mean clearer accountability, better value for money, and stronger leadership for our community.

I’m passionate about making a real difference for Eastleigh; I hope I can count on your support.

I am a local resident deeply rooted in Eastleigh, focused on our community, not personal ambition. Having lived here since I was seven, I am committed to serving those who call Eastleigh home.

My priority is improving quality of life, better healthcare, affordable housing, local jobs, and protecting our environment. Crucially, I believe in society's obligation to protect the vulnerable.

I aim to strengthen the Green Party's voice on the council. With representation, we can join more committees and deliver meaningful change for Eastleigh.

Together, we can build a greener, fairer future where no one is left behind.

profile.statement_to_voters:
I'm delighted to be standing for re-election as your Labour & Co-operative candidate in Passmores ward! I am seeking your support to re-elect me on Thursday the 7th of May as your local voice to represent you on Harlow Council.

In the two year term I served I've resolved various casework issues across the ward and made several contributions in the Council chamber as your elected representative. I've ensured I've worked to represent all residents in our ward, irrespective of who you voted for or whether you voted at all - that's my pledge to you - to work hard, for each and every resident.

Promoted by Stefan Mullard-Toal on behalf of Jake Shepherd, both at Unit 1, Whitehall Estate, Flex Meadow, Harlow, CM19 5TP.

I’m Stephanie Marsh – Local Community Champion & your candidate in the upcoming local election on May 7th.

I’ve worked as a Taxi driver locally for over 30 years, & for some while a part time carer too. Driving a taxi gives me an “ear on the ground” as to local issues that are affecting Harlow residents.

The upcoming election is between the Greens & Reform in Passmores.
Across the country, support for the old parties is dropping away whilst current Yougov polling shows the Greens & Reform surging in popularity.

Reforms campaigns focus largely on Nigel Farage & national issues like crime, immigration & Tax. Harlow Council is responsible for bin collections, planning permissions, environmental health, parks & leisure, & grass cutting etc.

The person you elect will only have an impact on local issues.

Labour are losing more & more support every day!
Time & again Labour has failed to stand up for the people who needed them most.
By abandoning communities & breaking their promises, they’ve created a vacuum in our politics that Nigel Farage & Reform are now rushing to fill with empty slogans & division.

I can win in Passmores.

Dan Swords is telling anyone who will listen to let him finish the job, the conservatives, currently have a majority of one on the council. Which in turn means they can ride roughshod over any differing view of their vision of how the town should look. By electing myself as your Green Councilor, his majority evaporates, this doesn’t throw everything in jeopardy, as Mr. Swords would have you believe. He will have to consider & work with other points of view & listen to the concerns the residents of Passmores may have as to the direction of travel.

Please consider voting for me.

Best Wishes
Stephanie

profile.statement_to_voters:
My name is Chris Jackson, and I have been a resident of Hull for two years. I love the vibe of this city and its rich history and diversity. I have built a 14-year career as an IT engineer and currently work for a local Hull firm.
I am a military veteran with nine years of service in the UK reserves. In my free time, I enjoy going on walks with my family, as well as following local and international politics and history.
I joined the Green Party because I believe that people need support just as much as our environment does. Many of society’s issues stem from decades of mismanagement by governments and a wealthy few not paying their fair share, while the media often blame the most vulnerable.

Hull’s potential has been squandered by mainstream parties that take people’s votes for granted. Together with my fellow Green Party councillors, I want to bring hope, action, and long-term
change to the ward and the wider city. With your vote, we can achieve this by providing better and greener transport links, building higher-quality social housing, retrofitting existing empty homes, creating purpose-designed green spaces, and supporting efforts to bring the cost of living down for all citizens of Hull. Vote for change, and together we can build a better Hull for the future.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Gareth has spent his entire adult life living and working in West Lancashire, volunteering within the local community and campaigning for improvements to residents’ lives.

Gareth has lived and worked in Ormskirk since 2008 and has been a Borough Councillor here since 2014. He has spent his life working and volunteering to support others, through his union, Labour, and co-operative work; for Alzheimer’s Society; serving in community roles including, school governor, on Ormskirk School Foundation Trust, Ormskirk School Local Academy Council and rugby club Director.

He also supports our local Credit Union where he is Vice Chair, South West Lancs Independent Community Advice Network (ICAN), and is a member of West Lancashire’s community renewable energy group – setup to reduce energy costs and invest in our community.

Passionate about Ormskirk, he is responsible for securing speedbumps on Cottage Lane, saving the red phone box in the town and widespread investment in local facilities from parks and playgrounds to town centre heritage and local healthcare settings.

Gareth says:
_“Over the past 12 years, I have worked closely with residents, community groups, fellow councillors, council officers and other organisations to bring about improvements to the lives of the people of Ormskirk.

“Through small interventions and partnership working, we have been able to bring about successes such as road resurfacing, infrastructure investment and town centre activities.

“I have lived in Ormskirk all my adult life and I have previously worked for Alzheimer’s Society, working in partnership with local agencies to set up Dementia Friendly West Lancashire in 2016.

“In the ward I have chaired the Police and Communities Together (PACT) meetings for many years, until police funding cuts abolished them, but still work closely with Lancashire Constabulary.

“And we continue to keep the developers at bay, protecting our green space and putting a stop to further student House in Multiple Occupation (HMO) conversions in the town since taking power in 2015.

“We have maintained our award-winning Green Flag Coronation Park, preserved our 700 year old charter market – even when opposition councillors talked about its closure, and kept Park Pool open.

“On 7 May, you will have the opportunity to use your vote for who you feel will best represent and serve your needs. I have a proven track record of standing up for local residents, putting residents first and foremost before any party politics.

“On the council, I have always been a team player and enjoy successful cross-party working on residents’ issues, such as working closely with the Conservative Leader to introduce a Public Space Protection Order (PSPO) to tackle drag racing and car meets locally; with Our West Lancashire (OWLs) on tackling fireworks nuisance and town centre car parking; and with Independent councillors to save our council’s housebuilding company.

“Even an OWL councillor notes, **“I was so pleased with the work Councillor Dowling put in to the car parking charges in Ormskirk”** and **“he works hard and I have respect for a lot of what he does and says”** in respect of my ability to work cross-party for the good of all residents.

“If re-elected, I will continue to be responsive to residents’ concerns and issues, and do my best to support and represent you. Use your vote on Thursday 7 May, to elect someone who stands up for you.”_

Aside from his council role, Gareth works in the Co-operative movement supporting local branches across the North West in delivering on key Co-operative campaigns including… 
-	Delivering food security 
-	Tackling modern slavery 
-	Challenging abuse of retail staff 
-	Protecting public spaces and community assets 
-	Supporting new co-operative and community-led housing 
-	Investing in renewable community energy schemes 
-	Promoting and developing credit unions

When not busy working or volunteering, Gareth can be found playing rugby for Ormskirk, playing 5aside football or running with local run clubs.

Independent candidate for Ormskirk West under the Our West Lancashire group. My focus is local issues that matter to residents, and with no party whip, I can vote freely to give residents a voice in their community. I’m local, approachable and willing to put the work in to make Ormskirk an even better place.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Chris has been a Party member for more than 20 years and served as a Trafford Councillor in Brooklands for more than 10 years, and was in 2022–23 Mayor of the Borough.

Service to the Community has for almost all his life been a passion, dating back to his university days when he started a Saturday morning Youth Club for local children at his Hall of Residence. Joining a Rotary Club in 1983, motto “Service above Self”, Chris has been continuously active since at Club, District and National level.

He has been a Governor at Sale High School and at Brooklands Primary School for over 10 years and was for many years Trafford’s ‘Appointed’ Governor at the Manchester NHS Foundation Trust Hospital Group

He is a founder member of The Friends of Moor Nook Park and sits on the Sale Moor Community Centre Committee and has also been involved in the Sale West Community Hub

In his spare time Chris has been active in local sport particularly Squash at Sale Rugby Club where he is a Vice President and also at Sale Golf Club serving for 14 years as Hon Secretary and then Chairman, and as President in 2020 – 2022. He is also currently President of Sale Photographic Society.

Dr. Anna Fryer has always been interested in public service – “Growing up in Cumbria, my Mum and Dad were always encouraging us to look after others – no wonder my three other sisters also became Doctors and Teachers!” Anna’s philosophy is simple- the reason I am standing is because I have spent 20 years working in the NHS and to make patients life better, we need more people like me with real experience in health in Parliament, who understand the NHS and patient care. The last few years in the NHS and Social Care have been a failure of Government. Quite simply our citizens have found the NHS has not been there for them when they needed it most. This is not the fault of NHS workers but a choice by the Conservatives not to plan our services and staffing properly. It has been heartbreaking watching patients waiting in the Emergency Department for hours and even days. Patients face huge barriers in accessing care, be it difficulties seeing their GP or long waiting lists. This is a failure that this government should be ashamed of. A vote for the Liberal Democrats is a vote to save our NHS after years of Conservative chaos. One issue that has been raised time and again by the people of Pendle and Clitheroe on the doorsteps is the horrifying conflict in the Middle East and the humanitarian catastrophe unfolding in Gaza. Anna says “I have spoken to many people about this conflict, and the unimaginable horror facing civilians. I have been profoundly moved by our own Liberal Democrat MP Layla Moran’s work and campaigning calling for an immediate, bilateral ceasefire. As the daughter of a Palestinian she has faced the trauma of supporting her family escape yet has spoken insightfully to understand both peoples. As Liberal Democrats we have not sought to play political games and given in to the siren song of easy answers. Instead we have stood consistently and clearly for a ceasefire in Gaza and an end to the suffering of innocent civilians.

Here's some info about me...... I currently live in Etching Hill and I have lived in Rugeley for the whole of my life.

Having been a District Councillor in the past, I understand what is needed to really represent our community and to stand up for residents. As a Councillor I was known for resolving issues raised by residents, holding local ‘help and advice’ sessions and carrying out ‘estate walks’ on the Springfields to identify and report issues.

By working with residents, together we addressed numerous issues in the area such as fly-tipping, housing repairs and overgrown trees and hedges.

I have lived in the area all my life and understand the challenges we have faced as a community over recent years, such as unemployment and deteriorating public amenities.

Supporting the community is important to me, advocating for residents’ concerns and supporting initiatives to help resolve the problems we face.

If elected, I aim to focus on listening, learning, and building constructive relationships – I recognise that people in the community often have the best solutions to problems, and that long-term success is achieved through co-operation and shared purpose.

My approach is guided by a focus on transparency, fairness, and practical outcomes to support strong communities and ensuring that people feel informed, respected and included in the processes that affect them.

Key Achievements

Contributed to community initiatives focused on improving local services and resident wellbeing, including promoting healthy cooking sessions and sharing energy saving ideas and initiatives.
Worked collaboratively with organisations to address neighbourhood priorities such as anti-social behaviour.
Promoted council transparency, by clearly explaining why decisions were being made and sharing this with residents, for example planning outcomes and budget announcements.
Championed practical, sustainable solutions shaped by residents’ feedback, including securing Council‑funded skips for the Springfields so that households without transport could safely and easily dispose of accumulated waste.
If elected, I would

Hold regular ‘help & advice’ sessions so that residents can meet with me and raise the issues that they regard as important.
Create opportunities for residents to take part in local community action such as public meetings to discuss big issues, and litter picks in ‘hot spots’.
Stand up for our area to get our fair share – actively engage with Government to gain funding to develop our area; ensure that planning applications include improvements to infrastructure and services; supporting local businesses; restoring local services; and improving public transport and the local environment.
On  Thursday, May 7th, 2026, I’m asking for your vote so I can have the opportunity to deliver real change for our community.

candidates.name: Kenneth John Paul George Richard Beardmore

profile.statement_to_voters:
Hello and welcome to my Reform UK page and the local issues my General Election campaign will focus on. 

Plymouth Independence Day

Vote Reform UK on the 4th July so Sutton and Devonport get an MP who is independent of the Labour Party, the Labour Council and the Conservative Police and Crime Commissioner.

Someone who will hold them to account and get things done. No more excuses, just action.

1. Safer streets for everyone.

2. Stop polluting our sea and rivers. 

3. A centrally funded toll-free Tamar.

4. Military pay and housing they deserve.

5. Transform empty offices into affordable homes.

6. Uncover the facts about the Armada Way budget. 

Why vote for me?

To help answer that I’d like to ask you to imagine you telling your friends and family that you're going to stand in the General Election to become an MP. 

If you did tell them, I'm sure you'd get a similar response to what I did (although there were other responses!).

"Wow, that's great, we need more “normal” people like you as an MP".

It's funny though isn’t it, how Keir Starmer and Rishi Sunak are both working so hard to reclaim their working-class roots, toning down their parent's work and showing the public how "normal" they are.  

I can't tone down my first job at the council as an apprentice vehicle mechanic fixing anything from lawnmowers to gritters. I can't even claim I was any good at it either which is why I was planning to get into sales as soon as I'd qualified. My Dad was in sales and he played golf every Friday so it looked like an easy option. But my Mum was a crazy artist and she encouraged me to go off travelling to "see the world". So I did. And survived. But there were some times when survival was looking a little less likely (I can bore you with my survival stories in the pub sometime.)

But I've done OK for a normal person. I did eventually get into sales, then fell into recruitment, then got wowed by the Internet in 1998, built a software business, sold it, lost it, started again with nothing, and managed to survive as a self-appointed Internet Recruitment Guru (I'd be known as an Influencer today!). 

In between all of my jobs, I did what Mum said and travelled. Australia, South America, North America, North Africa and more. I also somehow managed to find myself a wife and she even convinced me to settle down and have kids. But though my travel had been curtailed, I still managed to fit in numerous crazy ultra marathons in great places such as the Sahara desert, Amazon jungle, and the Yukon. 

But it's not all about me 

My wife and two daughters have all worked in the care sector; a community physio, a nurse and a Navy medic (although our daughters have picked up the wanderlust now). Having seen them all do work that has a real purpose (unlike selling software), and working throughout the pandemic, when we moved to Sparkwell in 2021 I decided to start working as a volunteer at the Plymouth Soup Run. To do something with purpose, to have a positive impact on those less fortunate.

Why homelessness? 

It all stems back to a travelling incident in Cusco, Peru in 1994 where I fed some homeless kids for a few days. It's another story for the pub but that experience left me regretting not being able to do more and since then I've always stopped and talked to street homeless people no matter where I am. 

Plymouth Soup Run

Life on the streets of Plymouth has got progressively worse since COVID and we have massively increased the number of meals we serve on a Sunday night to over 125. As the numbers increase it becomes far more tense for both volunteers and clients, and the streets feel less safe, so it’s vital that the police can provide a rapid response when (rarely) needed.

Do more

My first-hand experience on the streets of Plymouth made me realise to be able to do more, I had to do more. So I joined Labour in 2022 hoping I could do more, but that was not to be. So after almost 2 years with Labour, I resigned, thwarted at every step by party politics. Not the party or the people for me. All too angry with the Tories and no focus on the important issues. 

But I’m not someone who gives up easily or makes excuses so I researched Reform UK and realised they have policies I support. Common sense ideas that will make a difference. Not more wishy-washy flip-flop Labour twaddle or delusional Conservative cons. 

Which is why I am standing for Reform UK in Plymouth Sutton and Devonport.

Let’s be honest though; we've all lost trust in politicians who spend more time on political point-scoring and less time on real work. We need business people to fix the country, to hold the councils and the police to account, and to make sure our essential services such as water and housing are at a high standard to ensure the health and well-being of the community. This is why if I am elected I’ll be making LOCAL issues my priority, working with people, regardless of party, to get things done. No lame excuses, just hard work and focus.

You can find out more about my campaign plans by connecting with me on any of the social media channels below. You can also invite me to your pub for a beer and a few stories any time, and maybe even get a few people together to get more voters on our side.  

July 4th is Plymouth Independence Day

Vote Reform UK on the 4th July so Sutton and Devonport get an MP who is independent of the Labour Party, the Labour Council and the Conservative Police and Crime Commissioner.

Someone who will hold them to account and get things done. No more excuses, just action.

1. Safer streets for everyone.

2. Stop polluting our sea and rivers. 

3. A centrally funded toll-free Tamar.

4. Military pay and housing they deserve.

5. Transform empty offices into affordable homes.

6. Uncover the facts about the Armada Way budget. 

If you want results not excuses, vote for Peter Gold, Reform UK on July 4th.

profile.statement_to_voters:
After the recent Parliamentary win by Hannah Spencer of the Green Party in the Gorton and Denton by-election (26th February 2026) which was started due to the suspension of the sitting MP for sending offensive WhatsApp messages, it became clear to me that we all needed to stand-up and work together to bring back a level of trust, decency, honesty, and fairness to our communities.

Every day we’re bombarded with digital messages, print media, television, radio, and social media messages from other parties like Reform, what’s left of the Conservative party, a Labour party (sadly) in turmoil and disarray, and a Lib Dem party (usually strong in local elections), but falling behind in the polls. The messages are typically divisive, full of fear, division, blame, and hate. Well, to put it bluntly, I’m fed up of the stale, hate filled, blame filled, and (far too often) lies and fictional content that many of the parties that the other candidates are standing for represent, and are sadly not strong enough to call out.

I freely, and without any agenda admit that I don’t know any of the other candidates personally, or even through their political parties they represent, so I can’t and would never comment in any way as to their beliefs, actions, what drives them, what they like, dislike, or what their take is of the present state of the country; Stephen Cawley (Conservative), Ann Christine Fryer (Reform UK), Tony Hulme (Liberal Democrats), or Yavin Michael Owens (Labour), I’m sure, like me, all they all want to do is to make our communities better, offer support, and guidance to those they represent, and above all to be honest, trustworthy, dependable, caring, accountable, and true to their words they use when presenting themselves to the voters.

The present Councilor for the Ward; Stephen Cawley is liked on the Council, his attendance is consistent (always good to see), and I’m sure he’s a decent person who, during his time has represented the Ward well, and has been part of many local issues.  Ann Christine Fryer (Reform UK) candidate, again, I do not know Ann, and again, I’m sure she has given a lot to her community, her friends, and those around her.

We’re often told that local issues are not representative of national issues. That “pot holes, litter, dog poo bins, refuse collections” are local issues, and that we shouldn’t look at the bigger picture of what is transpiring in our communities, towns, counties, and country.

So, should we look at the local council elections on the 7th of May, 2026, simply as something small, something that will not make a difference to our lives, that your vote won’t change the rhetoric of the parties backed by billionaires, millionaires, and overseas lobbyists?

Let’s just take 5 minutes and look at some of the recent “events” that, although national issues’, quite literally affect everyone, especially from a local perspective directly;

We have the Sue Gray report, the Russia report, the COVID inquiry, Post Office scandal, PPE scandal (with over £4,000,000,000 wasted in PPE that could never be used), 232,112 COVID deaths while the Government partied, a Prime Minister whose disastrous budget cost us in excess of £30,000,000,000. Like me, I’m sure that Stephen Cawley looks at these facts as horrific, and knows exactly what our community lost in funding because of the obscene decisions made.

Reform portray themselves as the “People’s Party”. In their previous incarnation, they were UKIP, and they gave us Brexit. This has cost the UK somewhere between £140,000,000,000 and £240,000,000,000 (to date) Investment to the UK is between 12% and 18% lower, UK trade has been heavily impacted with studies suggesting goods exports are significantly lower. Independent analysis also shows that Public Finance has cost in excess of £90,000,000,000 a year.

In 2023, studies suggested around 1.8 million to 2 million fewer jobs existed in the UK compared to a scenario where Brexit did not occur

Did you know that Reform leader, Nigel Farage who portrays himself as a  “man of the people”, is actually a privately educated millionaire.

Did you know that Joint research by the Mirror and the Good Law Project in 2024 and early 2025 revealed that the Reform UK owner has four houses in the South of England as well as the one in Clacton he eventually acquired being the local MP (which is owned by his partner, no doubt for prudent financial reasons). That’s 5 houses…!

In 2013, The Mirror revealed that Farage had set up an offshore trust fund on the Isle of Man, in 2016, he also refused to release his tax returns, unlike a number of high-profile politicians, in the wake of the Panama Papers tax avoidance scandal. His huge earnings again came to light when his disclosures were made in the latest Register of Members’ Financial Interests, an obligation for any MP, showing he is the UK’s highest earning MP. Farage earns over £1.2m a year on top of his MP’s salary of £91,346. Remarkably, he has declared a £81,607 payment from GB News for 32 hours work, a rate of more than £2,500 an hour.

During the recent general election, a broad sweep of far right and extreme right groups and figures, including Tommy Robinson, Britain First, Patriotic Alternative and the Homeland Party, all urged their supporters to vote for Reform UK.

Farage is a longstanding critic of the NHS, and has argued that the UK should move to a private insurance-based health service. He said: “I think we’re going to have to think about healthcare very very differently and I think we’re going to have to move to an insurance - based system of healthcare.

So, as I said earlier; Ann Christine Fryer (Reform UK), I’m sure has given a lot to her community, her friends, and those around her, and is a valued member of the Eaton Socon community, but the political party she represents, do you, your families, your businesses, your communities truly want to be represented in any way by such a party?

These are some of the reasons why I am standing for the Green Party. I feel that it’s time to stand-up, to make a statement of intent that the direction our communities, and country has been heading towards, is not one that I want to know. So, here’s a few things about me …..

I was actually born in St Neots, at home. I grew up in Bedford, went through the comprehensive school system. My first job was in a Travel Agency in Bedford (Allen Sturges Travel. Sadly, no longer there). I’ve travelled greatly through work, as well as for leisure. Work wise (up until 2011) I was head of marketing and communications for an international consultancy. Then in 2011, things took quite a sharp turn (downwards).

On the morning of the 12th April 2011, I fell to the floor (at home) dead. I’d suffered a massive bilateral pulmonary embolism. The amazing East of England Ambulance service, and the NHS hospital (Hinchingbrooke NHS) truly stepped up, and they will always have my undying support, and admiration. Then in 2013, my wife and I were in a serious RTC by the A14 in Huntingdon. A chap in a Mercedes thought it a good idea to be using his phone as he drove down the slip road, straight into our broken-down car. Again, my never-ending thanks and support goes to the East of England Ambulance service, Cambridgeshire Fire service, Cambridgeshire Police, and Hinchingbrooke NHS. Suffice to say, all of these services that so many take for granted will have my full support if elected.

Workwise after two such severe incidents has been problematic. I wrote CV’s for a few years, and even supported youth offenders (previously in custody at Little Hey prison), with employment advice, CV writing, and acted as a mentor through the Trailblazers Charity.

In 2016 I started my own charity; “Charity Oars”, and raised a lot of public awareness of the signs and symptoms of thrombosis. The campaign was supported by many celebrities like; Joe Root, Devon Malcolm, Paul Grayson, Oliver Phelps, Liam Plunkett, Jonny Bairstow, Miles Jupp, Jack Russell, James Taylor, Tanni Grey Thompson, Steve Coogan, Martin Bayfield, Austin Healey, Helen Glover, Gordon Greenidge, James Phelps, Stephen Merchant, Simon Jones, Billy Connolly, Paul Young, Alec Stewart, Allen Lamb, Ashley Giles, Andrew Triggs Hodge, Dara O’Briain, Nick Robinson, Jos Buttler, Charles Spencer, Martin Corry, Ben Morgan, Doctor Dawn Harper, Fern Britton, Boris Johnson, Kumar Sangakkara, Matt Lucas, Michael Atherton, Jason Gillespi, Naseer Hussain, Rob Keys, Wojciech Szczesny, Gyles Brandreth, Mathew Pinsent and Clare Balding

https://www.rbrlegflow.com/charity-oars

Then in 2019 I started RBR legflow™, I even made it onto BBC’s Dragons Den (series 19, episode 9). So, not only are we now raising continued awareness for thrombosis (Deep Vein Thrombosis, and Pulmonary Embolism), but we’re preventing further deaths caused by blood-clots with the RBR legflow™. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nVyp8Da1nJk&t=175s

It’s been quite a journey to get here, where we are today. Did I ever see myself prior to 2011 undertaking all of the things that I have done since then, and would I have thought about standing for the Green Party in a local council election? Honest answer is probably not.

The truth is however; I have embraced every day, challenge, opportunity, and task with a new-found respect for those around me; and for those who work tirelessly (quite often behind the scenes) to make our communities safe, happy, welcoming, respected, and valued. So, for me to represent the community of Great Staughton, would be a privilege.

Thank you for taking the time to read my words …

Vote Paul James Westerman (the Green party) on the 7th May 2026

My wife and I moved to Woodley over 25 years ago to bring up our three children. They all attended local schools (Highwood, Bulmershe and Waingels). We found that Woodley has a great sense of community, some lovely open spaces, and is a lovely place to raise a family.

I’d be honoured to earn your support. You’ll see me around Loddon, ready to listen and stand with you. Together we can make honesty & hope normal again.

I'm standing as someone outside the political bubble - a working dad of two young children who cares about the kind of place families actually have to live in day to day.

I’m not here to make big promises I can’t keep, and I’m not pretending this is a safe Conservative seat. But I do think residents deserve someone willing to listen properly and speak honestly about what’s going wrong; whether that’s anti-social behaviour, poor planning decisions, or feeling like local voices are ignored.
Labour has held this area for years, and Reform will offer protest. I’m offering something simpler: a fresh set of eyes, a willingness to challenge decisions that don’t make sense, and a focus on the everyday issues people deal with on their own streets.

“A vote for the Greens is a vote for hope”.

I work to promote Greater Manchester as a great place to live, work, visit, and invest in. profile.statement_to_voters: My name is Anne Cullen and I am hoping to become your Labour and Cooperative Party Councillor for Calthorpe and Easington. I have lived in this area for the last 37 years. I am a registered social worker and I worked for Oxfordshire County Council Social Services serving children, young people and families in Banbury and the surrounding area. Later I worked in a large hospice providing care for dying people and their families. Nowadays I work part time supporting staff who work in the NHS, social care and hospices. I also work on voluntary basis to improve care for dying people who are homeless or living in poverty.  My professional career has been about service and I believe this experience has equipped me well to become an effective and committed councillor. Outside of work my husband and I love being busy grandparents and this is one of many reasons why I want to be part of building a better future for people of all ages in this area, including affordable housing and better transport, ending fuel poverty and protecting the environment.

I’m standing as the common-sense candidate for Cropredy, the Sibfords and Wroxton. Having worked in the NHS since 2012 with the vast majority of my career being in the Ambulance Service in North Oxfordshire, I’m a practical problem solver with true understanding of the real-life problems individuals and communities face on a daily basis. 
In my experience; division does not deliver. Inter-agency working is at the heart of what I do and so I am dedicated to representing you as a true believer in teamwork with you as the priority.
With your support I will bring an empathetic ear and a voice that delivers as a local person with a real stake in our area.

We at the SDP are very committed to providing greater focus to Family, Community and Nation. In order that we identify our worth and can build stronger ties in our community and to be a proud citizen of the United Kingdom.

I am a resident of Cropredy and have been for 8 years. I have been a serving police officer for 33 years of my life, a college lecturer, worked at both the Ashmolean Museum and Bodleian Library. During Covid I worked as a carer, and up until recently in reception/security at Wroxton College.

1. I believe education is such an important part of life, but we seem to be failing young people in schools and colleges, with a lack of discipline, funding, support and in so many cases lack of providing options for school leavers (although the latter also includes society in general), and not forgetting the most recent disaster, stating that our local children are not able to access Banbury schools and have to attend a school in Bicester. ACCOUNTABILITY!

2. The SDP are seeking public ownership of both the rail network and local water authorities. The cost of fares rising every year and lack of facilities on the trains is way out of control. The same of course can be said about the water authorities. The ultimate insult to injury, is the outrageous bonuses being paid to senior management, who have proven records of failing in their roles of providing safe waters to drink and swim in. ACCOUNTABILITY!

3. Affordable housing for many years was an issue for the towns & cities of our land, but now it is of great concern throughout the UK. New housing is of course a necessity, but to suggest it’s affordable is an insult to our intelligence. ACCOUNTABILITY!

4. Crime and Policing. In our rural communities we are constantly being made aware of incident's involving suspicious activity and people, via social media! We have definitely been left to fend for ourselves as a result of horrendous cut backs to policing (this like many other issues is another concern throughout the UK). The most insulting thing about all this, is the BS government and media keep pushing our way stating that “Crime is going down”. ACCOUNTABILITY!

5. Don’t get me started on Immigration. Only last week, I spent 8 hours with a friend who was having a medical procedure at the John Radcliffe Hospital, Oxford. The hospital was virtually full with immigrant staff, who happened to be very hard working, lovely and caring individuals, providing a fantastic service for the NHS and our community. They were not illegal immigrants, there is a difference! ACCOUNTABILITY!

Look, these are some of the pressing issues in our ward, so we need to ask more questions and demand satisfactory answers that can be tried & tested and fact checked. Join the SDP in seeking the facts and accountability.

Life is not all doom & gloom, of course there are great, great people and organisations doing the most fantastic jobs to help many of our community in every day scenarios, be that family members, teachers, carers, police officers, the doctors, nurses and  support staff of the NHS, local councillors, builders, tradesmen/women, refuse collectors, factory, retail & hospitality workers, young people and of course all the volunteers, in a nutshell the grafters and workers.
These are the same people having sleepless nights, as they struggle to give their family the best opportunities.

profile.statement_to_voters:
We the people of Makerfield have always worked hard, either in the mills or the mines throughout history or today trying to get by in this cost-of-living crisis. We are a special bunch; we know our worth but we aren’t getting what we deserve.

Our roads are full of pot holes, the hospital’s overflowing, people can't afford to get on the housing ladder, apprenticeships are hard to come by, drugs are rife and openly taken, pubs are closing down, parents have to work full time away from their kids 40 odd hours a week just to get by and many are struggling, pensioners are sat in cold houses scared to put the heating on because it costs too much, wokeness creeps into every aspect of our lives and our freedoms are being gradually taken away.

I'm not standing by whilst any of that happens, I'm taking a stand against it. Evil prevails when good men stand by and do nothing, I'll fight for what's right and do my bit.

I will fight tooth and nail in Parliament to ensure we get our worth from the tax money that we pay. I’ll work harder than any politician in the Country to ensure that we get what we deserve. The difference between me and a typical politician is I have done many a hard day’s work in my life, I know struggle from first-hand experience growing up in a single parent household on free school dinners.

I’m a plumber, I’ve served in the British Army, I worked for NHS for 6 years including throughout the pandemic, my family name can be traced back to living in Makerfield for at least the last 200 years, I'm a rugby league fan, a family man, a military man and a tradesman.

I don't like the way our Town and Country has gone since Tony Blairs government ruined the Country and then the Tories have carried it on to the mess we have now. I'm throwing my hat into the ring because I can't stand by idle anymore, I want to make Wigan/Makerfield better and despite what chance we may have or the obstacles we might face, I’m willing to get stuck in and do what’s right for the people of Makerfield.

When the next General Election is called, please have the courage to lend me your vote and I promise I will make Makerfield a better place to live for us all. We can change this, we can make Makerfield better, your vote can make that happen.

Vote Reform UK.

Hi, I'm Peter
I’ve always felt that a councillor should already be part of the community long before an election comes around. I didn’t wait for a title to start getting involved locally; I’ve raised my family here, and I’ve been putting the work in for years simply because I care about this place.

Some of the things I've already achieved for the community include lobbying the council for a pedestrian crossing on Wallsend Road - I'd witnessed numerous children and mothers with prams encountering near misses. The council listened and the crossing was finally installed in 2024! I also frequently report fly-tipping in the area and I have achieved numerous timely clean-ups by the council as a result.

This place matters to me. The people here matter. Families and community matter. And that’s why I’m standing for election - to make a real difference for the things that matter most.

A bit about me, I’m a firm believer in community. As your Green Party candidate and if elected, I’m here to listen to your concerns and issues and use my skills and determination to do my utmost on your behalf to create solutions. This will be working with all the different people, organisations and charities in Chirton and Percy Main to lever positive changes. profile.statement_to_voters:
https://www.rugbylabour.co.uk/manifesto/?

RUGBY LABOUR’S LOCAL ELECTION MANIFESTO LAUNCHED: ‘A COMMON SENSE PLAN FOR THE FUTURE OF OUR COMMUNITY’   • Common sense, pragmatic ideas to strengthen our economy and our community • 6 Pledges: 1. accessible housing 2. better infrastructure 3. safer neighbourhoods 4. a reimagined town centre 5. action on heritage 6. a green future Our Manifesto boosts the final stages of our local election campaign across all wards   Rugby Labour Party today launched its manifesto for the Rugby Borough Council elections to be held on 5 May. The document – ‘A Common Sense Plan For The Future Of Our Community’ – sets out six bold and costed pledges across a range of policy areas.   The manifesto explains how a Labour-run council would work to strengthen the community and the economy of the town and its neighbouring areas, after years of Conservative rule nationally and locally, that has left voters facing rising taxes, bills and crime, cuts to public services, a town centre abandoned to decline, and families £2,620 worse off as they face the Conservative Cost of Living Crisis.   On heritage, Labour would match residents’ pride in their town with a new vision of a friendly and diverse culture that encourages more visitors, businesses and events into the town through better use of existing spaces.   Labour plans a radical reimagining of the town centre that highlights Rugby’s unique character and celebrates its shops and boutique enterprises. The party would audit empty buildings to identify those that could be brought back into use in innovative ways to boost the local economy and regenerate the town, such as providing a hub for self-employed people, small businesses and others, and a separate hub where all council and voluntary services could be accessed in one place.   Labour would address the acute lack of housing for people wanting to get on the housing ladder or needing affordable or social rented accommodation. This includes establishing a Council-owned Homes for Rent company, working as a not-for-profit enterprise, renting properties at prices in line with current council properties, with the money being reinvested in future house building.   The manifesto sets out Labour’s ideas to tackle the lack of investment by Conservatives locally and nationally in adequate infrastructure for a rapidly growing town like Rugby. Labour will hold the Conservatives to account for failing to insist on sufficient roads, schools and health services and is demanding better regulation by the council of Houses in Multiple Occupancy in the town centre. The party will continue to call for services to be brought back to St. Cross Hospital and more locally-based ambulances.   Labour’s measures on the environment would boost the local economy and tackle climate change simultaneously, through buying council products from local suppliers to reduce carbon footprint, creating jobs and apprenticeships by insulating homes and switching to local energy producers. To tackle fly-tipping Labour aims to create local council funds for each council ward to provide support and equipment for local groups to carry out litter-picking and create community gardens. The party would expand, maintain and protect urban green spaces in part by creating a register of community assets.   To create safer neighbourhoods Labour aims to address problems caused by years of cuts to police under the Tories, by instigating a community partnership-based model for tackling crime. The party aims to establish community forums in different parts of the borough for residents, police and councillors to highlight crime and anti-social behaviour ‘hotspots’ in their local area. Labour will also build on its work taking community-led initiatives to key stakeholders, such as on restoring streetlights.

I’m proud to call Worthing home. After moving here two years ago, I quickly fell in love with this town, its people, and its potential. As a married woman working in the railway, I understand the importance of reliable services, strong communities, and being listened to.

I’m standing because I want to make Worthing even better, for everyone who lives here.

I am a fierce campaigner for clean waterways. The ongoing dumping of sewage into our sea is unacceptable. Water companies must be held accountable, and their profits should be reinvested into upgrading infrastructure—not lining pockets while our environment suffers.

I also care deeply about our local economy. Worthing thrives when independent businesses thrive. I want to support small, local enterprises and bring more life back to our high streets.

Community matters. Worthing should be a place where everyone feels safe, welcome, and respected. I stand firmly against racism and homophobia and will always champion equality and inclusion.

Beyond politics, I’m passionate about music and the role it plays in bringing people together. I want to see more opportunities for young people in Worthing to access, learn, and enjoy music, because every young person deserves a creative outlet and a sense of belonging.

I’m not just someone who lives in Worthing. I’m someone who wants to fight for it

profile.statement_to_voters:
My name is Kirsten Wiltshire, I’m 47 years old, married with 3 children and I’ve lived in and loved Fareham since I was 10 years old. I’ve only recently got into politics when I discovered my naïve view that the local council had our best interests at heart was a huge misjudgement! I feel that we no longer have a voice and my view is that the local areas should be run by the people who actually live there taking politics out of the equation. All residents should be listened to and heard, where change is needed it should be a collective decision by the residents directly affected. I have decided to stand as the Warsash Councillor for the Fareham Independent Group in the upcoming election and if you feel that your voice needs to be heard then vote for me & I will do everything in my power to make the changes necessary.

* Fighting for building genuinely affordable houses on brownfield sites and keeping the green spaces! * Lobby on local issues for local people - giving you your voice back! * Investigating transparency within the council ensuring accountability on what our local taxes get spent on!

I was born in Portsea and grew up in Leigh Park, where I experienced first-hand the strength of community spirit and the importance of looking out for one another. I moved to Fareham in 1987 and, over the past 39 years, have seen how our town has changed and grown.
Throughout my working life, I have been dedicated to caring for others, spending many years in both the private care sector and the NHS before retiring. This experience has given me a deep understanding of the challenges facing our health and care services, as well as the importance of compassion, reliability, and community support.
I have also worked as company secretary for my husband’s small business, where I saw the realities of running a business in today’s challenging economic climate. This has given me a strong appreciation for the pressures faced by local businesses and the vital role they play in our community.
As a mother of three and grandmother of seven, I care deeply about the future of our community. I am passionate about improving wellbeing, safety, education, and opportunities for the next generation.
I am proud to be standing as a Reform UK candidate for the Fareham Town ward. My priorities are to address crime, help residents feel safer in their neighbourhoods, and ensure the delivery of better, more reliable local services for everyone.

Hi, I’m Lucy and I’m standing to be re-elected as your Green Party councillor for Haldens on Thursday 7th May.

For the past eight years, it truly has been a privilege to serve as a councillor for Haldens. I am appealing to have your trust again so I can continue supporting the community in what looks to be a busy and difficult upcoming year, starting with the new Local Plan and Local Government Reorganisation.

As your local councillor, I will be able to achieve even more for you, from working closely with the county councillor to fix potholes and grass verges, to pushing for fairer and greener housing policies and building.

I have been utterly dismayed by reports of a lack of care towards the housing of vulnerable persons, whether that be through allocations or repairs. As I stated in the chamber, it is not good enough, and I will continue to push for better.

This election is really clear, it’s either Labour, or you can vote for the Green Party and me on Thursday 7th May to make hope normal again for Haldens and Welwyn Hatfield.

My passion is education and as a local teacher I am deeply invested in delivering for our area and making it the best it can be - for all generations. I always believe we should support every individual with the opportunity to be a valuable member of our community and ensure people have the skills they need to thrive.

I love our town and its unique character and as your Councillor, I want to focus on preserving the beauty of nature in Welwyn Garden City. We are lucky to live in such green and vibrant area and I am committed to protecting our local environment.

Our Community is special and deserves a dedicated voice at Welwyn Hatfield Borough Council and with your support, I will work hard for Haldens ward - listening, acting and standing up for our community.

She is quickly developing roots here, and cares deeply about making our community safe, green, and welcoming.

I feel that the old two party system has been letting us down as long as I've been alive and am keen to represent the Green party in our bold vision for a politics of hope that can steer our area and nation back on track. This way we can look after eachother in order to live comfortable lives.

profile.statement_to_voters:
My priorities:  I will back you and your family.  As your MP, I will:  1. Regenerate our area by boosting investment and jobs;  2. Cut the cost of living, by voting for measures which reduce tax and inflation;  3. Help small businesses by reducing red tape and taxes on business;  4. Reduce anti-social behaviour by working with the Police and the community;  5. Invest in health and social care, so we all get the care we need when we need it;  6. Protect our green spaces and our trees. 

About me: I previously worked in industry on the project of building full-fibre broadband right across the UK, dealing with the regulator, central and local government. I understand how critical connectivity is to our daily lives. I am now a solicitor, fighting for my clients every single day.

I have been a local Councillor, fighting hard for residents on issues such as speeding, cutting local crime and protecting green spaces. I was previously a Trustee of a local museum.

Coming from a small business background, I am particularly passionate about helping small businesses to grow, employ more people and succeed.

In my spare time, I am a runner. I have completed a marathon, half-marathon and several 10ks.

I recently retired from Brayleys Cars Ltd  where I was the founder and CEO. I led the growth from a single site operation in St Albans to a £400 million turnover multi franchised business  with 31 dealerships from the north of Manchester to the south of London. 
Through disciplined acquisitions, strong manufacturer partnerships and a relentless focus on operational performance , culture and governance.
I love living in Harpenden which I have done since 2014 and have a relationship with the town going back to 2011 when I turned the derelict site next to BP into the Kia car dealership it is today. Creating over 30 jobs locally along with a nice place to buy or have your car maintained !
So, having time on my hands I would now like to give back to the community I live in by applying the practical business skills I have developed over a lifetime, to partaking in the more effective running of our local council should I be so lucky as to be elected.
